definitions: sharp and biting in tone, wit with an acid edge
examples: Her acerbic commentary made the awards show watchable and made her few friends backstage.
He's funny, but his acerbic emails don't land well with new hires.
words.etymology: Latin acerbus, harsh and bitter like unripe fruit: speech that puckers the mouth.

definitions: Easy to talk to and pleasant to be around, the kind of person who makes conversation feel effortless.
examples: The new department head was so affable that even the interns who dreaded meetings started lingering in his doorway to chat.
She's affable with customers, which is half the reason they keep coming back.
words.etymology: From Latin affabilis, from ad plus fari, to speak to: literally someone you can speak to easily.

definitions: Up in the air, high above the ground or deck.
examples: The hawk hung aloft on the thermal, wings barely moving against the noon glare.
The winning captain held the trophy aloft for the cameras.
words.etymology: From Old Norse a lopt, into the sky, the same lopt that gives English loft.

definitions: Of a story: widely told but of doubtful truth, probably invented even if it deserves to be true.
examples: The tale of the professor failing his own exam question is almost certainly apocryphal, though every department claims it happened there.
That quote is apocryphal; Einstein never said it.
words.etymology: From Greek apokryphos, hidden away, from apo- plus kryptein, to hide: originally scriptures kept out of the official canon.

definitions: Greed for money or possessions taken to the level of a defining vice, wanting more purely for the having.
examples: The miser's avarice was such that he reused tea bags for a week while millions sat in his accounts.
The scandal exposed the plain avarice behind the charity's fees.
words.etymology: From Latin avarus, greedy, from avere, to crave.

definitions: Taken as self-evidently true, needing no proof; the kind of statement a system starts from rather than argues for.
examples: In that lab it was axiomatic that data beats intuition, so nobody bothered defending the claim, they just ran the experiment.
It's axiomatic that you back up your files before an update.
words.etymology: From Greek axioma, that which is thought worthy or fitting, from axios, worthy.

definitions: to talk loudly and threateningly with more wind than substance behind it
examples: The landlord blustered about eviction until the tenants produced the inspection report.
He blustered for a while, then quietly signed the agreement.
words.etymology: Probably from Low German blustern, to blow in gusts: the weather word for windy talk.

definitions: inflated, high-sounding language with far more volume than substance
examples: Strip away the bombast and his keynote said only that the company would try harder.
The speech was pure bombast, big words wrapped around a small idea.
words.etymology: Originally cotton padding used to stuff garments, from bombax (cotton): speech stuffed with filler.

definitions: Shameless and bold about something most people would at least try to hide.
examples: The thief was brazen enough to sell the stolen paintings back to the gallery's own curator.
That was a brazen lie, and everyone in the room knew it.
words.etymology: From Old English braesen, made of brass: a brazen face is as hard and unblushing as the metal.

definitions: A harsh, jarring mix of sounds all happening at once.
examples: Dawn in the port was a cacophony of gulls, winches, and men shouting over diesel engines.
The middle school band produced a cheerful cacophony during warmups.
words.etymology: From Greek kakos, bad, plus phone, sound: literally bad-sound.

definitions: to coax someone into something with flattery, charm, and gentle persistence
examples: It took an hour of cajoling before the retired engineer agreed to look at the failed weld.
She cajoled her brother into driving her to the airport at 5 a.m.
words.etymology: French cajoler, to coax; one old story ties it to the chattering of a caged jay.

definitions: Honest, open speech that says the uncomfortable thing plainly instead of dressing it up.
examples: With surprising candor, the CEO admitted the product had shipped a year too early.
I appreciate your candor, even if the feedback stings.
words.etymology: From Latin candor, whiteness or radiance, from candere, to shine: honesty as a kind of brightness.

definitions: Belonging to the accepted core of a field, the standard version everyone is measured against.
examples: The syllabus stuck to the canonical texts, so the students met Kant and Hume but never the cranks and heretics who made them necessary.
That's the canonical way to set up the config file; the wiki shows three worse ones.
words.etymology: From Greek kanon, a measuring rod or rule, later the church's official list of scriptures.

definitions: Governed by whim, changing direction suddenly and without any pattern you can plan around.
examples: The capricious director rewrote the ending four times in one week, once because of a dream.
Mountain weather is capricious, so pack for everything.
words.etymology: From Italian capriccio, a sudden shiver or whim, folk-associated with capra, the goat, an animal that leaps unpredictably.

definitions: To scold or punish someone sharply for a fault, with the sting meant to correct them.
examples: The coach chastised the entire defense at halftime, naming each missed assignment.
She chastised herself for forgetting the anniversary.
words.etymology: From Latin castigare, to correct or make pure, built on castus, pure: to chastise was to purify by discipline.

definitions: Rude and graceless in a sour, ungenerous way, especially when a little courtesy would cost nothing.
examples: It felt churlish to complain about the free meal, but the waiter had dropped it in front of us without a word.
He gave a churlish shrug when the volunteers thanked him.
words.etymology: From Old English ceorl, a low-ranking freeman or peasant: churlish originally meant having peasant manners.

definitions: So clear and logically forceful that it compels agreement; an argument that pushes you to its conclusion.
examples: Her dissent was the only cogent thing said all afternoon, and by evening three committee members had quietly switched sides.
He made a cogent case for renting instead of buying, and I couldn't argue with it.
words.etymology: From Latin cogere, to drive together or compel, from co- plus agere: a cogent argument drives you along with it.

definitions: To interpret words or actions in a particular way, to take a meaning from something that could be read differently.
examples: The court construed the comma in clause four to mean the tenants owed nothing, and forty years of leases shifted overnight.
Don't construe my silence as agreement.
words.etymology: From Latin construere, to pile up or build, from com- plus struere: to construe is to build a meaning out of the pieces.

definitions: Genuinely sorry for what you did, with the remorse showing in your manner rather than just your words.
examples: The contrite teenager returned the borrowed car washed, fueled, and with a handwritten apology on the dash.
He seemed contrite about missing the deadline, so we let it go.
words.etymology: From Latin conterere, to grind down or crush: a contrite heart is one crushed by remorse.

definitions: To back up a claim with independent support, making it stronger; what a second witness or a second dataset does.
examples: The satellite imagery corroborated the villagers' account, and the story went from rumor to front page.
Can anyone corroborate that you were home by ten?
words.etymology: From Latin robur, oak or strength: to corroborate is literally to strengthen.

definitions: a political speaker who wins crowds by stoking their fears and resentments instead of reasoning with them
examples: The senator had become a demagogue, promising the mill town its jobs back while naming a new villain every week.
Every crisis produces at least one demagogue who profits from the panic.
words.etymology: Greek demos (people) plus agogos (leader): originally just a leader of the people, until too many led them by the nose.

definitions: to talk down someone or something, deliberately painting it as worthless
examples: The campaign spent millions denigrating a rival it publicly claimed not to fear.
I'm not denigrating your work, I'm asking for one revision.
words.etymology: Latin denigrare, to blacken thoroughly, from niger (black): to paint a reputation black.

definitions: Dried out completely, with all moisture and often all life gone.
examples: The lakebed was a desiccated plain of cracked hexagons stretching to the mirage line.
The recipe calls for desiccated coconut, not the fresh kind.
words.etymology: From Latin de plus siccus, dry: thoroughly dried.

definitions: a long, bitter verbal attack that keeps going well past the point of persuasion
examples: What began as a toast curdled into a diatribe against the groom's entire profession.
He launched into a ten-minute diatribe about parking enforcement.
words.etymology: Greek diatribe meant a wearing away of time, a discourse: the lectures got angrier and the word followed.

definitions: Designed to teach a lesson, sometimes to a fault: preachy, morally instructive, treating the audience like pupils.
examples: The novel collapses in its final act into a didactic sermon, each character reduced to a mouthpiece for the author's politics.
His toast got a bit didactic, more lecture than celebration.
words.etymology: From Greek didaskein, to teach, the same root that gives us didactics, the science of teaching.

definitions: someone who dabbles in an art or field for pleasure without ever committing to real rigor
in its older, kinder sense, a lover of the arts
examples: The gallery owner dismissed him as a dilettante who bought a kiln, fired six lopsided bowls, and moved on to fencing.
I'm a dilettante at chess, I know the openings and nothing else.
words.etymology: Italian, literally one who delights, from dilettare, from Latin delectare, to charm.

definitions: Having no personal stake in the outcome and therefore impartial; often confused with uninterested, which means bored.
examples: They needed a genuinely disinterested arbiter, someone who owned no shares, owed no favors, and feared neither side.
A referee should be disinterested, not indifferent.
words.etymology: Built from dis-, apart from, plus interest: standing apart from any stake in the result.

definitions: to speak of someone or something as inferior, chipping away at its standing
examples: He disparaged the whole genre at dinner, unaware his host had written three of its bestsellers.
Don't disparage the offer until you've heard the details.
words.etymology: Old French desparagier, to marry beneath one's rank, from par (equal): to treat as not your equal.

definitions: asserting opinions as unquestionable truths, deaf to evidence or debate
examples: The professor grew dogmatic in his last decade, grading down any student who questioned his pet theory.
She's too dogmatic about diet to have a real conversation with.
words.etymology: Greek dogma, an opinion or decree, from dokein (to seem good): the opinion that forgot it was one.

definitions: deliberate two-facedness, saying one thing while working toward its opposite
examples: The memo exposed years of duplicity: public pledges of safety, private orders to skip the inspections.
She was hurt less by the breakup than by the duplicity that preceded it.
words.etymology: Latin duplex, twofold: being double, one face for the audience and one for the plan.

definitions: To make something murky clear, usually by explaining it; to shed light on.
examples: The footnote elucidated in two lines what the professor had failed to explain in two lectures.
Could you elucidate what you meant by 'flexible deadline'?
words.etymology: From Latin lux, light, via lucidus, clear: to elucidate is to bring light to a thing.

definitions: a heavy, listless boredom with life itself, the flavor of tedium that comes from having nothing you want
examples: Three weeks into the yacht trip, a gray ennui settled over the heiress that no amount of champagne could lift.
By February the whole office had sunk into ennui.
words.etymology: French, from the same Latin phrase behind English annoy, in odio, in hatred, boredom as a mild form of loathing.

definitions: Lasting only a very short time, here and gone before you can hold onto it.
examples: The frost flowers on the window were ephemeral, gone by the time she had fetched her camera.
Fame on the internet is ephemeral; last month's sensation is this month's trivia question.
words.etymology: From Greek ephemeros, lasting a day, built from epi (upon) and hemera (day), like a mayfly's whole life.

definitions: Steadiness of mind under stress, taking good and bad news with the same level calm.
examples: The surgeon received the malpractice verdict with the same equanimity she brought to the operating room.
He handles criticism with surprising equanimity for someone so young.
words.etymology: From Latin aequus, even, plus animus, mind: an even mind.

definitions: To use deliberately slippery, ambiguous language so you never quite commit to a position; to dodge with words.
examples: Asked point-blank whether the layoffs were coming, the CEO equivocated for six minutes without producing a single verifiable sentence.
Stop equivocating and tell me yes or no.
words.etymology: From Latin aequus, equal, plus vocare, to call: to call two different things by the same name.

definitions: Deeply and widely learned, with the kind of knowledge that comes from years among books.
examples: The bartender turned out to be startlingly erudite, quoting Thucydides from memory while pulling a pint.
Her erudite footnotes are half the pleasure of the book.
words.etymology: From Latin e-, out of, plus rudis, rough or untrained: the erudite person has been polished out of rawness.

definitions: So light, delicate, and otherworldly that it seems to belong to the air rather than the earth.
examples: Mist rose off the lake in ethereal ribbons that dissolved before they reached the pines.
The soprano's ethereal high notes made the hall go completely still.
words.etymology: From Greek aither, the pure upper air the gods breathed, above the ordinary atmosphere of mortals.

definitions: to urge someone strongly and earnestly toward an action, cheerleading with force
examples: The old foreman exhorted the crew to check every bolt twice, as if the bridge's ghosts were listening.
The letter exhorts alumni to donate before the deadline.
words.etymology: Latin exhortari, an intensified form of hortari (to encourage): encouragement turned up to full volume.

definitions: To extend a pattern beyond the data you actually have, inferring the unknown from the known; risky exactly in proportion to how far you stretch.
examples: From three data points the intern extrapolated a hundred-year trend, and the room went quiet in the wrong way.
Don't extrapolate from one bad date to a doomed relationship.
words.etymology: A 19th-century coinage built on interpolate, swapping extra-, outside, for inter-, between: estimating outside the range instead of within it.

definitions: a thing already done and presented afterward, so that objecting is pointless
examples: By the time the board convened, the merger was a fait accompli, signed over the weekend without them.
He moved the furniture while I was out and presented it as a fait accompli.
words.etymology: French for accomplished fact, a deed sealed before anyone could argue.

definitions: Smelling foul, with the heavy stench of rot or stagnation.
examples: The canal ran fetid and slow beneath the old slaughterhouse, and even the gulls avoided it.
Someone left the gym bag in the car all week and now the whole trunk is fetid.
words.etymology: From Latin fetere, to stink.

definitions: Sad and abandoned-looking, left behind and pitifully alone.
Nearly hopeless, as in a forlorn attempt.
examples: A forlorn bus shelter stood at the edge of the moor, its timetable bleached blank by years of sun.
The dog gave a forlorn whine as the family car pulled away.
words.etymology: From Old English forloren, utterly lost, the past participle of forleosan, to lose completely.

definitions: socially clumsy in a way that makes onlookers wince, lacking the instinct for what a situation calls for
examples: Asking the widow how much the estate was worth struck everyone at the funeral as breathtakingly gauche.
It felt gauche to mention money at the dinner, so I let it drop.
words.etymology: French for left, the awkward hand in a right-handed world, so the left-handed became the socially clumsy.

definitions: Actually relevant to the matter at hand, bearing on the point rather than merely near it.
examples: The senator's fishing anecdote, whatever its charms, was not germane to the appropriations bill under debate.
Keep your comments germane to the agenda, please.
words.etymology: From Latin germanus, having the same parents; Shakespeare stretched 'german' from kinship of blood to kinship of subject matter.

definitions: fluent and effortless in a way that suggests no real thought or sincerity underneath
examples: The salesman had a glib answer for every objection, which was exactly what worried her.
That's a glib response to a serious question.
words.etymology: Probably from Low German glibberig, slippery: talk that slides out too easily.

definitions: sly cleverness in deceiving or outmaneuvering people
examples: She won the estate fight not with lawyers but with guile, letting her cousins overplay their greed in front of the judge.
He's honest to a fault, no guile in him at all.
words.etymology: Old French guile, deceit, from a Germanic source and cousin to the English word wile.

definitions: to lecture someone loudly, aggressively, and at length, whether they want it or not
examples: The coach harangued the referees until security walked him to the tunnel.
Stop haranguing me about the dishes, I said I would do them.
words.etymology: From Medieval Latin harenga, a speech to an assembly, likely from a Germanic word for the ring of listeners gathered around the speaker.

definitions: Open, uncultivated land covered in low shrubs like heather and gorse.
examples: Wind moved over the heath in long purple waves, and there was no other living thing in sight.
The trail crosses two miles of open heath before the woods.
words.etymology: From Old English haeth, untilled land, the root hiding inside heathen, a dweller on the wild heath.

definitions: A practical rule of thumb or mental shortcut that usually gets you a good-enough answer fast, without guaranteeing a correct one.
examples: The old engineer's heuristic, double the estimate and add a week, outperformed the project-management software every quarter.
A decent heuristic for wine is to buy the second-cheapest bottle.
words.etymology: From Greek heuriskein, to find, the root Archimedes shouted in the bath as eureka.

definitions: Overweening pride or self-confidence, the kind that classically invites a downfall.
examples: It was pure hubris to launch the rocket without the third round of tests, and the fireball proved it.
The startup's collapse was a case study in founder hubris.
words.etymology: From Greek hybris, insolence or outrage, the arrogance that offended the gods in Greek tragedy.

definitions: Someone who attacks cherished beliefs and revered institutions; originally, a literal smasher of religious images.
examples: The department hired an iconoclast who opened her first lecture by calling the field's founding study unreplicable folklore.
He fancies himself an iconoclast, but mostly he just contradicts people.
words.etymology: From Greek eikon, image, plus klastes, breaker: the Byzantine militants who smashed icons in the eighth century.

definitions: Bossy in a grand, commanding way, issuing orders as if obedience were owed to you.
examples: With an imperious wave, the diva sent the entire string section back to the top of the score.
Her imperious tone in emails rubs the new hires the wrong way.
words.etymology: From Latin imperium, command or supreme authority, the root of empire and emperor.

definitions: Glowing with heat, shining because it is literally or figuratively burning.
White-hot with emotion, especially rage or brilliance.
examples: The forge door swung open and the room filled with incandescent orange light.
She was incandescent with anger when she saw the invoice.
words.etymology: From Latin candere, to glow white, with in- as an intensifier, kin to candle and candid.

definitions: Lazy in a settled, comfortable way, avoiding effort as a lifestyle rather than a lapse.
examples: The indolent heir spent his thirties perfecting the art of the eleven a.m. breakfast.
The heat made everyone indolent, and the project slipped another week.
words.etymology: From Latin in plus dolere, to feel no pain: it began as a medical word for painless conditions before drifting to painless living.

definitions: an indirect hint, usually damaging or suggestive, that lets the speaker imply what they dare not state
examples: The profile never accused her of anything, it just stacked innuendo until readers drew the conclusion themselves.
The office gossip traded in innuendo rather than facts.
words.etymology: Latin innuendo, by nodding at, from innuere (to signal with a nod): accusation delivered as a wink.

definitions: insulting, abusive language, the artillery of an argument that has given up on persuasion
examples: The pamphlet was pure invective, comparing the mayor to every tyrant from Nero onward.
He unleashed a stream of invective at the umpire.
words.etymology: Latin invehi, to ride against, attack: literally being carried into the enemy, with words for cavalry.

definitions: Quick to anger, with a fuse so short that small annoyances set off real flares of temper.
examples: The irascible chef hurled a saucepan across the kitchen because the parsley was chopped too coarse.
My grandfather gets irascible when anyone touches the thermostat.
words.etymology: From Latin irasci, to grow angry, built on ira, anger, the same root as ire.

definitions: Shimmering with rainbow colors that shift as the angle of light changes, like oil on water or a beetle's shell.
examples: The tide pool held an iridescent film of oil, beautiful and wrong at the same time.
She picked an iridescent thread for the embroidery so it would catch the light.
words.etymology: From Greek iris, iridos, rainbow, after Iris, the goddess who walked the rainbow between gods and mortals.

definitions: traveling from place to place for work rather than staying settled
examples: An itinerant knife sharpener still works these villages, ringing a bell from a bicycle heavy with whetstones.
She spent her twenties as an itinerant English teacher across three continents.
words.etymology: Latin iter, journey, via itinerari, to travel: a person defined by the road.

definitions: using the fewest possible words, clipped to the point of dryness
examples: Asked how the summit had ended, the general gave a laconic reply: badly.
His texts are laconic, one word when three would be friendly.
words.etymology: From Laconia, the Spartan homeland, whose people were famous for answers of brutal brevity.

definitions: to criticize someone harshly and at length, a verbal thrashing
examples: The food critic lambasted the flagship restaurant so thoroughly that reservations dried up within a week.
The coach lambasted us for the sloppy second half.
words.etymology: Likely a doubling of two old verbs for beating, lam and baste, so a beating twice over, transferred to words.

definitions: Slow, heavy, and unhurried, drained of energy in a way that can feel either sickly or luxurious.
examples: The afternoon heat made the whole village languid, dogs sprawled in doorways and shutters half closed.
He gave a languid wave from the hammock and went back to his book.
words.etymology: From Latin languere, to be faint or weak, the same root that gives us languish.

definitions: Heavy, gray, and dull as lead, said of skies, limbs, and moods.
examples: They marched under a leaden sky that promised snow and delivered only cold.
Her legs felt leaden on the last mile of the race.
words.etymology: From lead the metal, Old English lead, gray and heavy since before English was written.

definitions: Generous and big-hearted toward people you could easily punish or resent, especially rivals and those who wronged you.
examples: In a magnanimous gesture, the champion praised the challenger who had trash-talked her all season.
It was magnanimous of him to cover his ex-partner's share of the debt.
words.etymology: From Latin magnus, great, plus animus, soul or spirit: literally great-souled.

definitions: at the point of death, or so far in decline that the end is only a formality
examples: The mall was moribund, two stores open, fountains dry, an escalator running for no one.
The committee has been moribund for years, it meets but decides nothing.
words.etymology: Latin moribundus, from mori, to die: dying built into the word itself.

definitions: A conclusion that simply does not follow from what came before; also any jarring conversational leap out of nowhere.
examples: Her closing argument was a non sequitur so complete that the judge asked the stenographer to read it back, just to be sure.
He answered my question about rent with a non sequitur about his fantasy football team.
words.etymology: Latin for 'it does not follow,' lifted whole from the vocabulary of classical logic.

definitions: so eager to please and obey that it becomes fawning and faintly disgusting
examples: The maitre d' turned obsequious the moment he recognized the actress, bowing her past the waiting line.
His obsequious emails to the boss made the whole team cringe.
words.etymology: Latin obsequi, to comply (ob- plus sequi, to follow): one who follows after you a little too closely.

definitions: Unhealthily pale, drained of color.
Lacking vigor or intensity, a washed-out version of what it should be.
examples: A pallid moon hung over the marsh, giving just enough light to make the mist look solid.
The sequel was a pallid imitation of the original film.
words.etymology: From Latin pallere, to be pale, the root that also gives us pale and pallor.

definitions: flamboyant confidence of style, doing a thing with a flourish that makes it look like a performance
examples: She parallel parked the moving truck in one motion, stepped out, and took a small bow, pure panache.
He answered the tough questions with real panache.
words.etymology: From Italian pennacchio, the plume of feathers on a helmet, the swagger of the feather becoming the swagger of the person, a sense cemented by Rostand's Cyrano de Bergerac.

definitions: to give an audience whatever base thing it wants, principles be damned, in exchange for its approval
examples: The network pandered to outrage because outrage kept the ratings up.
Politicians pander to whichever crowd is in front of them.
words.etymology: From Pandarus, the go-between who arranges the love affair in the Troilus story: first a procurer, then anyone who services low appetites.

definitions: Obsessed with small rules and technical correctness at the expense of the actual point; showing off learning nobody asked for.
examples: He derailed the strategy meeting with a pedantic ten-minute correction about whether the chart's axis should say median or midpoint.
I know it's pedantic, but the recipe says simmer, not boil.
words.etymology: From Italian pedante, a schoolmaster, the fussy classroom teacher turned into an adjective.

definitions: a person no longer welcome somewhere, officially or socially frozen out
examples: After the exposé ran, the reporter was persona non grata at every restaurant the family owned.
Show up late twice and you're persona non grata with that group.
words.etymology: Latin for an unwelcome person, borrowed from diplomacy, where it is the formal label for an expelled envoy.

definitions: Sulky and snappish over trivial slights, like a child denied dessert.
examples: The star grew petulant when the interviewer asked about the flop, crossing his arms and giving one-word answers.
Don't get petulant just because we picked a different restaurant.
words.etymology: From Latin petulans, forward or insolent, from petere, to attack or to seek.

definitions: To calm someone's anger, usually by giving them something or soothing their pride.
examples: The manager placated the furious guests with a suite upgrade and a bottle of champagne.
A quick apology placated her, and the meeting moved on.
words.etymology: From Latin placare, to soothe or appease, cousin to placidus, calm.

definitions: a remark so worn and obvious it adds nothing, usually delivered as if it were wisdom
examples: The CEO answered the layoffs question with platitudes about journeys and family until the room went quiet.
His advice was all platitudes, follow your heart, trust the process.
words.etymology: French plat (flat): a flat remark, coined on the model of latitude.

definitions: A fierce written or spoken attack on a position or person; argument as combat rather than conversation.
examples: What began as a book review swelled into a forty-page polemic against the entire field of evolutionary psychology.
Her post wasn't analysis, it was a polemic, and it read like one.
words.etymology: From Greek polemos, war: a polemic is argument conducted as warfare.

definitions: to hold forth pompously on a subject as if your opinion were official doctrine
examples: Uncle Ray pontificated about cryptocurrency to a table of people who had stopped listening at the salad course.
He loves to pontificate about wine he has never tasted.
words.etymology: From Latin pontifex, the Roman high priest: to speak as though you were the pope pronouncing on doctrine.

definitions: To put forward an idea as a starting assumption or working claim, to be tested or built on rather than proven up front.
examples: The model posits that voters punish incumbents for local weather, an assumption the second chapter spends eighty pages defending.
Let's posit that we have the budget; what would we build first?
words.etymology: From Latin ponere, to place, in its participle form positus: to posit is to set a claim down on the table.

definitions: Seeming to know the future before it arrives; showing foresight that events later prove right.
examples: Her 2019 memo warning about supply chains looked paranoid then and prescient two years later.
Buying the generator turned out to be prescient; the power failed that same week.
words.etymology: From Latin prae, before, plus scire, to know: knowing beforehand.

definitions: nobly idealistic in a way that ignores reality, chasing a beautiful goal that cannot survive contact with the world
examples: His quixotic plan to hand-deliver an apology to every customer he had ever overcharged lasted exactly one city block.
It's a quixotic campaign, but I admire the candidate for running it.
words.etymology: From Don Quixote, Cervantes's knight who charged windmills he took for giants.

definitions: someone with a gift for telling stories aloud, the person a dinner party arranges itself around
examples: Uncle Silas was the family raconteur, able to stretch a flat tire in Ohio into twenty minutes of suspense.
She is a natural raconteur, so we let her tell the story of the trip.
words.etymology: From French raconter, to tell or recount, so literally a teller.

definitions: Deep, long-held bitterness toward someone, the kind of resentment that has had years to ferment.
examples: Even at the funeral, the brothers' rancor over the farm kept them on opposite sides of the chapel.
The debate ended without the usual rancor, which surprised everyone.
words.etymology: From Latin rancere, to stink or be rotten: rancor is resentment gone rancid.

definitions: Dazzlingly bright and splendid, dressed or lit to impress.
examples: The autumn maples stood resplendent against the slate sky, every leaf a struck match.
The groom arrived resplendent in a deep blue suit.
words.etymology: From Latin resplendere, to shine back brightly, from splendere, to shine.

definitions: Cheerfully confident that things will turn out well, even when the evidence is mixed.
examples: Despite the third rejection letter that month, she stayed sanguine, taping each one above her desk like a trophy.
Analysts remain sanguine about the company's chances next quarter.
words.etymology: From Latin sanguis, blood: in medieval humor theory, people dominated by blood were ruddy and cheerful.

definitions: mocking in a dry, bitter way, humor with a curled lip behind it
examples: Her sardonic toast, to the groom and whichever bridesmaid comes next, emptied half the room.
He watched the meeting fall apart with a sardonic smile.
words.etymology: From Greek sardonios, tied by ancient writers to a Sardinian plant whose poison supposedly twisted the face into a death grin.

definitions: Submissive to the point of having no self-respect, behaving like a servant even when nothing requires it.
examples: The courtiers' servile bowing continued long after the king had left the room.
He's helpful without being servile, which is a rare balance in that office.
words.etymology: From Latin servus, slave: servile behavior is literally the behavior of a slave.

definitions: To wrap or hide something completely, the way fog swallows a coastline or a sheet covers a body.
examples: By nightfall the fog had shrouded the lighthouse until only its horn proved it existed.
The project was shrouded in secrecy for two years.
words.etymology: From Old English scrud, a garment, so to shroud is to clothe something out of sight.

definitions: Full of smooth curves and turns, winding like a snake or a slow river.
examples: A sinuous path of stepping stones wound through the moss garden toward the tea house.
The mountain road is sinuous enough that the bus takes an hour to cover ten miles.
words.etymology: From Latin sinus, a curve or fold, the same word behind the sinus cavities that curve through the skull.

definitions: Deep, full, and resonant in sound, the kind of voice or tone that fills a room.
examples: The cathedral bell rolled its sonorous note across the rooftops and out over the harbor.
The narrator's sonorous voice made even the phone book sound important.
words.etymology: From Latin sonor, sound, from sonare, to make a sound, kin to sonic and resonate.

definitions: Looking convincing on the surface while being wrong underneath; an argument dressed up nicely enough that you almost miss the flaw.
examples: The consultant's slide deck was specious from start to finish: gorgeous charts, confident arrows, and not one number that survived a second look.
His excuse sounded fine at first, but it was specious, and everyone knew it by lunch.
words.etymology: From Latin speciosus, good-looking, from species, appearance: a specious claim is literally one that merely looks good.

definitions: Ghostly, pale, and unsettling, as if belonging to an apparition.
examples: Spectral shapes of frost-killed sunflowers lined the lane, bowing as the car lights passed.
The abandoned pier looks spectral in the early fog.
words.etymology: From Latin spectrum, an appearance or apparition, from specere, to look.

definitions: Fake or not what it claims to be; often said of reasoning, data, or correlations that have no real basis.
examples: The paper was retracted once reviewers showed the headline correlation was spurious, an artifact of two datasets sharing a clock.
Ignore that spurious claim about the deadline; nobody in the meeting ever said Friday.
words.etymology: From Latin spurius, an illegitimate child, extended to anything of false parentage or origin.

definitions: Hot and humid in an oppressive, heavy way.
Suggesting smoldering passion, as in a sultry voice.
examples: The sultry August night pressed on the city, and every window in the tenement stood open.
A sultry afternoon like this calls for iced tea and no plans.
words.etymology: Derived from swelter, which traces to Old English sweltan, to die, heat strong enough to kill.

definitions: someone who flatters the powerful relentlessly to gain favor, a professional yes-man
examples: The new director surrounded himself with sycophants who applauded every reorganization before reading it.
He's not a friend, he's a sycophant angling for a promotion.
words.etymology: Greek sykophantes, literally a fig-shower, originally an informer in Athens; how figs got involved is still debated, but the servility stuck.

definitions: Habitually quiet by temperament, saying little not out of shyness but as a settled way of being.
examples: The taciturn ranger answered our week of questions with maybe forty words, all of them useful.
He's taciturn at parties but writes long, warm letters.
words.etymology: From Latin tacere, to be silent, the same root as tacit.

definitions: Saying the same thing twice dressed up as insight, like 'it is what it is'; in logic, a formula that comes out true no matter what you plug into it.
examples: The mission statement was pure tautology, promising that the company would succeed by being successful.
Saying the winner is whoever wins is just a tautology.
words.etymology: From Greek tauto, the same, plus logos, word: saying the same thing twice.

definitions: Reckless nerve, the gall to do or say something that ignores obvious risk or offense.
examples: The rookie had the temerity to correct the general's map reading in front of the whole staff.
He had the temerity to ask for a raise after missing three deadlines.
words.etymology: From Latin temere, blindly or rashly: temerity is acting as if you could not see the danger.

definitions: Stormy and violent, whether describing weather or a relationship that behaves like weather.
examples: They crossed the strait in tempestuous seas, green water breaking over the bow.
Their tempestuous partnership produced three albums and two lawsuits.
words.etymology: From Latin tempestas, storm or season, itself from tempus, time, because weather was the mood of the times.

definitions: A state of physical or mental sluggishness, as if the body has been dialed down to its pilot light.
examples: A midwinter torpor settled over the garrison, men sleeping through the gray afternoons.
After the holiday feast the whole family sank into a pleasant torpor.
words.etymology: From Latin torpere, to be stiff or numb, the same root as torpid.

definitions: Shaking or quivering slightly, often from nerves, weakness, or strong feeling.
examples: Her tremulous candle flame threw shivering shadows up the stairwell.
In a tremulous voice, he asked if the results were final.
words.etymology: From Latin tremere, to tremble, the same root as tremor and tremendous.

definitions: Cutting straight to the heart of a matter; sharp, vigorous, and effective, usually said of criticism or analysis.
examples: One trenchant question from the back row undid an hour of the minister's careful evasions.
Her review was brief but trenchant: two paragraphs, no survivors.
words.etymology: From Old French trenchier, to cut, the same blade that gives us trench.

definitions: To move or be shaped in smooth rising and falling waves.
examples: The wheat undulated in the wind like a slow golden sea running to the horizon.
The road undulates through the foothills, so pack motion sickness pills.
words.etymology: From Latin unda, wave, so to undulate is literally to move wave-like.

definitions: Impossible to defend or keep holding, whether a position in an argument or a situation in life.
examples: After the third witness contradicted him, his version of events became untenable and his lawyer asked for a recess.
Working two jobs on four hours of sleep was untenable, so she quit one.
words.etymology: From Latin tenere, to hold, via French tenir: an untenable position is one that cannot be held.

definitions: polished and effortlessly courteous in company, with the smoothness of someone at home anywhere
examples: The urbane consul defused the shouting match with a joke in three languages and a signal for more wine.
He's urbane enough to make small talk with anyone at the reception.
words.etymology: Latin urbanus, of the city, city polish set against rustic manners.

definitions: to swing back and forth between choices, unable to settle on one
examples: She vacillated between the two apartments for so long that both were rented out from under her.
He vacillates about every menu until the waiter comes back twice.
words.etymology: Latin vacillare, to sway or totter, the physical wobble becoming a wobble of the will.

definitions: Lush and green with growing plants.
examples: After the rains the hills turned verdant almost overnight, every gully running with new grass.
They picnicked on a verdant stretch of lawn behind the library.
words.etymology: From Old French verdoyant, becoming green, ultimately from Latin viridis, green.

definitions: Driven by a desire for revenge, keeping score and making people pay for slights.
examples: The vindictive landlord kept the deposit over a nail hole, purely because the tenants had complained to the city.
She's not vindictive; she just wants an apology and then she moves on.
words.etymology: From Latin vindicta, vengeance, the root that also gives us vindicate.

definitions: speech or writing so corrosively hostile it seems designed to burn rather than argue
examples: The comment section filled with vitriol within an hour of the verdict.
Their divorce was conducted with surprising vitriol on both sides.
words.etymology: The old name for sulfuric acid, from Latin vitrum (glass): corrosive speech named after the stuff that eats metal.

definitions: Quietly longing for something out of reach, with a gentle sadness mixed into the wanting.
examples: He grew wistful at the smell of woodsmoke, remembering autumns at his grandfather's cabin.
She gave a wistful look at the pastry case and ordered the salad.
words.etymology: From the older English adverb wistly, meaning intently, with its sense pulled toward wishful by sound and association.

definitions: a person who achieves dazzling success very young, a prodigy already outperforming the adults
examples: The label signed the sixteen-year-old wunderkind who had produced three platinum tracks from a laptop in her bedroom.
He was the office wunderkind, running a team at twenty-four.
words.etymology: German Wunder (wonder) plus Kind (child): a wonder-child.

definitions: the mood, taste, and preoccupations that define a particular era, the invisible weather everyone in a period lives inside
examples: The film flopped in 1998 but nailed the zeitgeist of 2020, when everyone suddenly understood quarantine dramas.
Her podcast works because she has an uncanny feel for the zeitgeist.
words.etymology: German Zeit (time) plus Geist (spirit): literally the spirit of the age, a coinage beloved of German Romantic philosophers.

definitions: restrained in eating and drinking, holding back where others indulge
examples: The abstemious critic ordered sparkling water at the four-hour tasting menu, to the sommelier's quiet despair.
He's abstemious during the week and saves wine for Sundays.
words.etymology: Latin abs (away from) plus temetum, an old word for strong drink: keeping clear of the wine.

definitions: Hard to understand because it is dense, technical, or remote from ordinary experience.
examples: The proof was so abstruse that only three people alive could referee it, and two of them were feuding.
The manual's abstruse instructions defeated everyone in the house.
words.etymology: From Latin abstrudere, to push away and hide, from abs- plus trudere, to thrust: abstruse ideas are thrust out of easy reach.

definitions: To bring forward a fact, example, or piece of evidence in support of an argument.
examples: For her claim that the collapse was foreseeable, she adduced three internal memos and a whistleblower's calendar.
Can you adduce a single example of that policy working?
words.etymology: From Latin adducere, to lead toward, from ad- plus ducere: to lead evidence up to the argument.

definitions: Hostile feeling or ill will toward someone, often the hidden motive behind an action.
examples: The judge found the firing was driven by personal animus, not performance.
I bear him no animus; we just work differently.
words.etymology: From Latin animus, mind or spirit: in English the word narrowed to the hostile turn of mind.

definitions: Confident self-assurance, especially grace kept in a tricky or embarrassing moment.
examples: When the podium collapsed mid-speech, the mayor stepped over it with perfect aplomb and kept talking.
She fielded the hostile questions with aplomb.
words.etymology: From French a plomb, according to the plumb line: perfectly vertical, hence perfectly poised.

definitions: A formal written defense of one's beliefs or conduct; not an apology but its opposite, a justification.
examples: Rather than resign quietly, the minister published a fifty-page apologia recasting every scandal as a misunderstood act of courage.
The blog post read less like an explanation and more like an apologia.
words.etymology: From Greek apologia, a speech made in one's defense, as in Plato's account of Socrates on trial.

definitions: Strikingly appropriate to the situation; not just relevant but well chosen.
examples: She closed the eulogy with an apposite line from the letters he wrote at sea, and the room exhaled as one.
His example was apposite: a bridge that failed for exactly the reason we're discussing.
words.etymology: From Latin appositus, placed near, from ad- plus ponere, to place: set right next to the point.

definitions: With a sideways look of suspicion or disapproval; to view something askance is to distrust it.
examples: The old fishermen looked askance at the influencer's brand-new waders and thousand-dollar rod.
Regulators view the merger askance.
words.etymology: Attested in English since the 1500s; despite many guesses, its origin remains unexplained.

definitions: Harshness or sharpness of tone or temper, roughness in how something is said.
examples: The old professor answered with such asperity that the student never raised his hand again.
There was an edge of asperity in her reply to the third reminder email.
words.etymology: From Latin asper, rough, originally of surfaces before it described tempers.

definitions: a damaging remark that spatters doubt on someone's character, usually in the phrase 'cast aspersions'
examples: Each candidate cast aspersions on the other's war record while their own gathered dust unexamined.
I'm not casting aspersions, I'm just asking where he was that night.
words.etymology: Latin aspergere, to sprinkle: originally a sprinkling of holy water, later a sprinkling of mud.

definitions: A person who taught themselves, building real expertise without formal schooling.
examples: The lab's best statistician was an autodidact who had learned measure theory from library books between night shifts.
He's an autodidact; everything he knows about carpentry came from trial, error, and one battered manual.
words.etymology: From Greek autos, self, plus didaktos, taught: the self-taught one.

definitions: kind and genially protective in the manner of a favorite uncle
examples: The avuncular doorman kept spare umbrellas and unsolicited advice for every tenant under thirty.
The anchor's avuncular style made bad news easier to hear.
words.etymology: Latin avunculus, specifically the maternal uncle, the family's designated indulgent adult.

definitions: the person or thing you particularly detest and cannot stop being annoyed by
examples: Escalator etiquette is his bête noire, and he will narrate every violation on the ride up.
Budget meetings are my bête noire.
words.etymology: French for black beast, the creature you dread meeting.

definitions: flattering words or attentions deployed to coax someone into something, usually in the plural
examples: Deaf to the recruiter's blandishments, she asked only about the lab equipment and the data rights.
No amount of blandishment would get the cat out from under the bed.
words.etymology: Latin blandiri, to flatter, from blandus (smooth, caressing): smoothness weaponized.

definitions: a person devoted to the good life, especially fine food, wine, and lively company
examples: A lifelong bon vivant, he planned entire vacations around a single table he wanted to eat at.
She's the bon vivant of our group, always booking the next dinner.
words.etymology: French, literally a good liver, one who lives well.

definitions: Warm, easy friendliness, the good-natured glow of pleasant company.
examples: The pub's bonhomie survived even the quiz-night scoring disputes.
There was real bonhomie at the reunion, not just polite smiles.
words.etymology: From French bonhomme, good man: bonhomie is the quality of a thoroughly good fellow.

definitions: To strip a text of anything deemed offensive, usually clumsily and to its detriment.
examples: The school edition bowdlerized the novel so thoroughly that the murder at its center became a vague disagreement.
Streaming services quietly bowdlerize old comedies and hope nobody notices.
words.etymology: From Thomas Bowdler, whose 1818 Family Shakespeare cut every line he thought unfit for Victorian parlors.

definitions: dazzling technical boldness in performance, skill shown off at full volume
examples: The pianist closed with a bravura passage so fast the page turner simply gave up and stood back.
It was a bravura bit of lawyering, the whole case turned in one cross-examination.
words.etymology: Italian for bravery or great skill, from bravo, the same word audiences shout at the end.

definitions: Charmingly rural, evoking idealized countryside of pastures, herds, and slow afternoons.
examples: The brochure promised a bucolic retreat, all stone walls, grazing sheep, and no cell signal.
They traded the city for a bucolic life that turned out to involve a lot of fencing repairs.
words.etymology: From Greek boukolos, cowherd, from bous, ox, so the word literally smells of cattle pasture.

definitions: To polish something until it glows.
To improve or shine up a reputation or image.
examples: Evening light burnished the copper rooftops until the whole quarter seemed to smolder.
The charity gala did more to burnish his image than his actual giving did.
words.etymology: From Old French burnir, a form of brunir, to make brown or polish, from brun, brown.

definitions: a small secret group scheming together, usually for political power
examples: A cabal of junior officers had been meeting in the map room for months before the coup.
He talks about the school board as if it were a cabal.
words.etymology: From Hebrew kabbalah, the received mystical tradition, via French cabale: secret doctrine shading into secret club.

definitions: Young and untested, with the naivety of someone who hasn't been knocked around by experience yet.
examples: The callow lieutenant saluted everything that moved, including the regimental goat.
I was callow enough back then to think talent alone would carry me.
words.etymology: From Old English calu, bald: it first described unfledged birds without feathers, then unfledged people.

definitions: a false statement crafted to destroy someone's reputation, slander at its most deliberate
examples: The pamphlet's calumnies followed the doctor for years after the inquiry cleared her.
He dismissed the rumor as calumny spread by a jealous rival.
words.etymology: Latin calumnia, a false accusation or legal trickery, the same root that gives us challenge.

definitions: a false story or claim, often deliberately spread, that keeps circulating no matter how often it is debunked
examples: The candidate revived the old canard about the reservoir being sold to foreign investors.
That statistic is a canard; nobody can find its original source.
words.etymology: French for duck, from an old idiom about half-selling a duck, that is, swindling someone: the duck became the hoax itself.

definitions: clever case-by-case moral reasoning used to rationalize whatever you already wanted to do
examples: Through some impressive casuistry, the board concluded that the bribes were technically consulting fees.
It takes real casuistry to call that promise unbroken.
words.etymology: From Latin casus, a case: the theologians' art of resolving hard moral cases, which slid into a name for excusing anything.

definitions: Deep sky blue, the saturated blue of a perfect clear day.
examples: The trawler worked alone on a cerulean sea, the only object in all that blue.
She painted the nursery ceiling cerulean with little white clouds.
words.etymology: From Latin caeruleus, sky blue, from caelum, sky.

definitions: deception by clever trickery, especially the legalistic, fine-print kind
examples: Through some ballot-order chicanery, the incumbent's name appeared first in every county.
The lease was full of chicanery designed to eat the deposit.
words.etymology: French chicaner, to quibble or raise petty legal objections: trickery born in the courtroom.

definitions: Hot-tempered and easily provoked, with anger as your default weather.
examples: The choleric colonel turned crimson and snapped his riding crop over a mislabeled crate.
Her choleric streak comes out in traffic.
words.etymology: From Greek chole, bile: in humor theory an excess of yellow bile made you fiery and quick to rage.

definitions: talking around a point in many words instead of saying it plainly
examples: The memo's circumlocutions took three paragraphs to admit that the product had caught fire.
Ask him the time and you get five minutes of circumlocution.
words.etymology: Latin circum (around) plus loqui (to speak): speech that orbits the point without landing.

definitions: the people in the know within a field, the insiders whose taste defines what counts
examples: The ramen shop had no sign and no menu, which naturally made it a shrine for the city's food cognoscenti.
Among the sneaker cognoscenti, that release was old news by lunch.
words.etymology: Italian, those who know, from Latin cognoscere, to come to know.

definitions: an awkward clash or minor dispute that briefly derails the social machinery
examples: There was a contretemps at the buffet when two aunts reached for the last profiterole and neither would yield.
We had a little contretemps with the landlord over the deposit.
words.etymology: French contre (against) plus temps (time), originally a mistimed step in fencing or dance, a move against the beat.

definitions: To argue against or dispute a claim; to deny with reasons.
examples: No one has managed to controvert her central finding, though three labs have spent years trying.
The dates on the receipts controvert his whole story.
words.etymology: From Latin contra, against, plus vertere, to turn: to turn against a claim, the root of controversy.

definitions: A small stand of trees or bushes, a pocket-sized wood.
examples: A copse of birches stood white against the plowed field like a gathering of ghosts.
The deer bed down in the copse behind the barn.
words.etymology: Shortened from coppice, from Old French copeiz, a cut-over woodland, from couper, to cut.

definitions: Contemptibly cowardly, with a spinelessness that invites scorn rather than sympathy.
examples: The craven captain took the first lifeboat and radioed his regrets from a safe distance.
It was a craven decision to blame the intern.
words.etymology: From Middle English cravant, defeated, probably from Old French cravanter, to crush.

definitions: A grumpy, ill-tempered person, usually older, who complains as a hobby but is sometimes secretly beloved.
examples: The neighborhood curmudgeon glowered at the block party for an hour, then quietly fixed every kid's bike chain.
He's a curmudgeon about new music but he'll drive you to the airport at 4 a.m.
words.etymology: Attested since the 1570s with no confirmed origin; centuries of guessing have produced no proven source.

definitions: To mark with spots or patches of shifting light and shade.
examples: Sun through the beech canopy dappled the trail so that the whole path seemed to breathe.
Afternoon light dappled the kitchen wall through the blinds.
words.etymology: Possibly from Old Norse depill, a spot or small pool, the mark a drop leaves.

definitions: required by fashion, custom, or etiquette, the thing you simply must do or wear to belong
examples: At the gallery opening, black clothing and a faint air of boredom were de rigueur.
A thank-you note is still de rigueur after an interview.
words.etymology: French, literally of strictness: what strict propriety demands.

definitions: to speak or recite in a loud, formal, theatrical manner, as if addressing a crowd even when there isn't one
examples: He stood on the cafe chair and declaimed his manifesto to eleven bored regulars.
Students once learned rhetoric by declaiming classic speeches from memory.
words.etymology: Latin declamare, from clamare (to shout): practiced shouting, the ancient rhetoric student's homework.

definitions: Reasoning that advances through the clash of opposing positions, each answer generating a sharper question; argument as an engine of truth rather than a contest.
examples: The seminar worked by dialectic: she would state a thesis, he would demolish it, and the class would rebuild something better from the rubble.
Their marriage runs on a friendly dialectic of proposal and objection.
words.etymology: From Greek dialektike techne, the art of conversation, from dialegesthai, to converse.

definitions: So fine and sheer that light passes through it, said of fabrics, mists, and anything gauzy.
examples: Diaphanous curtains breathed in and out of the tall windows with the harbor wind.
She wore a diaphanous shawl over the gown, more suggestion than garment.
words.etymology: From Greek dia, through, plus phainein, to show: something that lets the light show through.

definitions: Hesitant and self-doubting, holding back because you don't quite trust your own worth.
examples: The diffident postdoc mumbled her breakthrough into her scarf, and the room nearly missed it.
He's diffident about his cooking, but the food says otherwise.
words.etymology: From Latin diffidere, to distrust, from dis plus fidere, to trust: diffidence is distrust turned on yourself.

definitions: Rambling from topic to topic rather than marching in a straight line; in philosophy, proceeding by reasoning rather than by intuition.
examples: His lectures were gloriously discursive, wandering from Roman aqueducts to jazz to his aunt's divorce before circling back, somehow, to the thesis.
The email was too discursive; I couldn't find the actual request.
words.etymology: From Latin discurrere, to run about, from dis- plus currere, to run: discursive prose runs all over the field.

definitions: Fond of arguing for its own sake; inclined to turn any exchange into a debate.
examples: The seminar's most disputatious member once contested the date printed on the syllabus.
Our book club got disputatious after the second bottle of wine.
words.etymology: From Latin disputare, to weigh up or argue, from dis- plus putare, to reckon.

definitions: To hide your real feelings, motives, or knowledge behind a false front; to lie by performance rather than by statement.
examples: She dissembled beautifully at the reception, toasting the merger she had spent all night trying to kill.
He's incapable of dissembling; his face announces everything.
words.etymology: From Latin dissimulare, to disguise or conceal, via Old French: to make a thing seem other than it is.

definitions: Rigidly committed to a theory or ideology regardless of whether it fits the facts on the ground.
examples: The new director was doctrinaire about open offices even as productivity fell and headphones bloomed on every desk.
He's too doctrinaire to admit the policy failed in this one case.
words.etymology: From French doctrinaire, a person of doctrine, first flung at a school of post-Napoleonic French politicians who governed by theory.

definitions: Sweet and soothing to hear, now often used with a wink, as in dulcet tones.
examples: The shepherd's pipe sent its dulcet phrases drifting down the valley at dusk.
I was woken at six by the dulcet tones of a leaf blower.
words.etymology: From Old French doucet, a diminutive of doux, sweet, from Latin dulcis.

definitions: Insolent nerve, the shameless audacity to do something outrageous without blushing.
examples: After crashing the wedding, he had the effrontery to complain about the seating chart.
She had the effrontery to bill us for her own mistake.
words.etymology: From Latin effrons, literally without forehead: in Roman idiom the forehead was where shame showed, so no forehead meant no blush.

definitions: Carrying the mournful, backward-looking tone of a lament for what is lost.
examples: The documentary takes an elegiac look at the fishing villages emptied by the collapse of the cod.
There was something elegiac about the last home game before the stadium was demolished.
words.etymology: From Greek elegos, a mournful song or lament, the ancestor of elegy.

definitions: To drain something of strength and vitality, leaving it limp. Note: it means the opposite of energize.
examples: Weeks of siege heat had enervated the defenders more than any assault.
The long commute enervates her before the workday even starts.
words.etymology: From Latin enervare, to cut the sinews out of, from e, out, plus nervus, sinew.

definitions: a person of refined and demanding taste in food and drink, pleasure pursued with expertise
examples: A true epicure, he crossed the city in the rain because one stall grilled its eel over the right charcoal.
She's an epicure about coffee, beans weighed to the gram.
words.etymology: From Epicurus, the Greek philosopher of modest pleasures whom later centuries recast as a patron of fine dining.

definitions: a short, polished saying with a twist of wit at the end
examples: Wilde could compress a whole philosophy into an epigram and make you laugh while swallowing it.
The chapter opens with an epigram about money and friendship.
words.etymology: Greek epigramma, an inscription (epi, upon, plus graphein, to write): wit compact enough to carve in stone.

definitions: Having to do with knowledge itself: what we know, how we know it, and how confident we are entitled to be.
examples: The two camps no longer share an epistemic foundation; they cannot even agree on what would count as evidence.
Admitting you might be wrong is basic epistemic hygiene.
words.etymology: From Greek episteme, knowledge, from epistasthai, to know how or understand.

definitions: being an inferior substitute for the real thing, imitation with the seams showing
examples: The theme park served an ersatz Paris, plastic bistro chairs under a half-scale iron tower.
The lobby's ersatz marble fooled no one who touched it.
words.etymology: German Ersatz, replacement, seared into English by the ersatz coffee and ersatz bread of wartime shortages.

definitions: A long, steep cliff or slope separating two levels of land.
examples: The road climbed the escarpment in eleven switchbacks, the plain unrolling gold behind them.
Hawks ride the updrafts along the escarpment all afternoon.
words.etymology: From French escarpement, from escarper, to cut steeply, from the same Italian scarpa that gives us scarp.

definitions: Fading from sight or memory almost as soon as it appears, like breath on glass.
examples: The aurora was evanescent, green curtains that dissolved each time she raised the camera.
Childhood friendships can be evanescent once families move away.
words.etymology: From Latin evanescere, to vanish, from e, out, plus vanus, empty, the same root as vanish.

definitions: to criticize someone so savagely it strips the skin off
literally, to abrade or wear away skin
examples: The inspector general's report excoriated the agency, naming names on every page.
Critics excoriated the sequel as a cash grab.
words.etymology: Latin excoriare, to strip off the hide, from corium (leather): criticism as flaying.

definitions: A close, careful interpretation of a text, drawing the meaning out line by line; the classic mode of scriptural and literary scholarship.
examples: His exegesis of the poem's third stanza took two seminars and overturned thirty years of received readings.
The podcast is basically an exegesis of one Springsteen album.
words.etymology: From Greek exegeisthai, to lead out or explain, from ex- plus hegeisthai, to lead: drawing meaning out of the text.

definitions: Low, waterlogged marshland, often fed by mineral-rich groundwater.
examples: Lanterns were forbidden on the fen at night, for the old folk said lights out there answered back.
The trust is restoring the fen for cranes and marsh orchids.
words.etymology: From Old English fenn, marsh, a word old enough to name whole English regions, the Fens.

definitions: Irritable and hard to manage, quick to quarrel or act up, like an overtired toddler or a divided committee.
examples: The fractious coalition splintered over the wording of a single footnote.
The kids got fractious around hour six of the drive.
words.etymology: From the old sense of fraction meaning discord or brawling, ultimately from Latin frangere, to break.

definitions: Crumbly, breaking apart easily under the fingers, said of soil, rock, and old paper.
examples: The cliff was friable sandstone, and every handhold came away in ochre crumbs.
Work compost in until the soil is dark and friable.
words.etymology: From Latin friare, to crumble.

definitions: to protest or denounce loudly and explosively, thundering against something
examples: The bishop fulminated against the new tax from the pulpit while his clerks quietly paid it.
Dad fulminated about the referee for the entire drive home.
words.etymology: Latin fulminare, to strike with lightning, from fulmen (thunderbolt): denunciation as weather event.

definitions: so excessive and gushing in praise that it feels insincere
careful writers reserve it for flattery gone rancid, though many now use it to mean simply abundant
examples: The introduction was so fulsome the honoree visibly winced at the podium.
His fulsome apology somehow made things worse.
words.etymology: Middle English, full plus -some: it first meant abundant, then the abundance curdled into cloying excess.

definitions: a persistent critic who irritates the powerful with uncomfortable questions, and considers it a public service
examples: The council dreaded the retired auditor, a gadfly who read every appendix and timed his questions for the cameras.
Every city hall needs a gadfly or two.
words.etymology: A biting fly that goads livestock, from gad (a spike): Socrates called himself the gadfly stinging the lazy horse of Athens.

definitions: To deny or contradict, usually a claim or evidence; mostly met in the phrase 'cannot be gainsaid.'
examples: The results were replicated in four independent labs, and even her fiercest rivals could not gainsay them.
There's no gainsaying that the plan saved money.
words.etymology: From Old English gegn-, against, plus say: literally to speak against.

definitions: talkative to the point of exhaustion, especially about trivial things
examples: The garrulous cab driver narrated the entire history of the bridge, twice, before they crossed it.
Grandpa gets garrulous after his second coffee.
words.etymology: Latin garrire, to chatter, a word the Romans coined to sound like the babble it names.

definitions: A whole you perceive in one grasp, where the arrangement itself carries meaning the separate pieces do not.
examples: Taken separately the clues meant nothing, but the detective suddenly saw the gestalt: every alibi depended on the same clock.
Don't fixate on one metric, look at the gestalt of the quarter.
words.etymology: German for shape or form, carried into English by the Gestalt school of psychology, which studied how minds perceive wholes.

definitions: The near-weightless spider silk that drifts through autumn air, and by extension anything impossibly thin and delicate.
examples: The meadow at sunrise was strung with gossamer, every strand beaded with dew and burning silver.
The scarf was pure gossamer, so light she forgot she was wearing it.
words.etymology: From Middle English gossomer, likely goose summer, the warm autumn spell when geese were eaten and drifting spider silk filled the air.

definitions: using extravagant, high-flown language meant to impress rather than inform
examples: The dedication plaque was so grandiloquent that visitors assumed the fountain did something more than gurgle.
His grandiloquent toasts go on twice as long as they should.
words.etymology: Latin grandis (great) plus loqui (to speak): great-speaking, with the greatness all in the speaking.

definitions: A biography so admiring it borders on worship; originally, the formal lives of saints.
examples: The authorized biography is pure hagiography, two decades of board meetings rendered as miracles.
The obituary skipped hagiography and told the truth, which he would have preferred.
words.etymology: From Greek hagios, holy, plus graphein, to write: writing about the saints.

definitions: Belonging to a remembered golden time of peace and calm, usually in the phrase halcyon days.
examples: He wrote of the halcyon summers before the war, when the worst news was a rained-out regatta.
Those were the halcyon days of the startup, before the layoffs.
words.etymology: From Greek alkyon, the kingfisher, which legend said nested on a sea the gods becalmed for fourteen days each winter.

definitions: Icy, aristocratic arrogance in one's bearing, superiority worn like perfume.
examples: The maitre d' surveyed our sneakers with a hauteur usually reserved for health inspectors.
Her hauteur melted the moment her old roommate walked in.
words.etymology: From French haut, high, from Latin altus: loftiness made into a manner.

definitions: Departing from accepted doctrine or mainstream opinion in a field.
examples: Her heterodox view, that the epidemic had two separate origins, was mocked for a decade and then quietly adopted by everyone who had mocked it.
He holds heterodox opinions about coffee: instant, he insists, is better.
words.etymology: From Greek heteros, other, plus doxa, opinion: holding an opinion other than the official one.

definitions: Theatrically overdramatic, performing emotions for an audience rather than simply feeling them.
examples: Her histrionic collapse onto the chaise longue was somewhat undercut by her checking her phone mid-swoon.
Let's skip the histrionic resignation threats and talk about the schedule.
words.etymology: From Latin histrio, actor: histrionics are stage behavior dragged into real life.

definitions: Gray or white with age or frost.
So old and overused it has whiskers, as in a hoary joke.
examples: A hoary oak guarded the crossroads, its bark silvered and its roots heaving the old flagstones.
He opened with a hoary pun that still somehow got a laugh.
words.etymology: From Old English har, gray and venerable with age, the same word hidden in hoarfrost.

definitions: a messy, embarrassing entanglement of misunderstandings and conflicting interests that is hard to escape
examples: The seating-chart imbroglio drew in three families, two ex-spouses, and eventually the caterer as a neutral party.
The licensing imbroglio delayed the launch by a quarter.
words.etymology: Italian, from imbrogliare, to tangle or confuse, a knot of threads turned into a knot of people.

definitions: To pester someone with persistent, badgering requests that will not take no for an answer.
examples: Fans importuned the retired singer at every cafe until she finally moved to a village with no train station.
He importuned the professor for an extension three times in one day.
words.etymology: From Latin importunus, unfit or troublesome, originally describing a place with no harbor: an importunate person gives you nowhere to dock.

definitions: To attack the truth or integrity of something, especially someone's motives or honesty.
examples: The senator did not answer the report; he impugned the auditors, their funding, and their haircuts.
I'm questioning the numbers, not impugning your honesty.
words.etymology: From Latin impugnare, to attack, from in- plus pugnare, to fight, from pugnus, fist: to throw a punch at a claim.

definitions: Only just begun and not yet formed; an idea, feeling, or institution still in its shapeless early stage.
examples: The manifesto captured the movement while it was still inchoate, all grievance and energy with no program.
I have an inchoate sense that something's wrong with the plan, but I can't name it yet.
words.etymology: From Latin incohare, to begin: the inchoate thing has been started but not finished.

definitions: Too great, subtle, or strange to be put into words; beyond what language can carry.
examples: Mystics keep writing books about the ineffable, a paradox none of them seems to mind.
There was an ineffable sadness in the empty house.
words.etymology: From Latin in-, not, plus effari, to speak out, from ex- plus fari: that which cannot be spoken.

definitions: a breezy, unbothered lack of concern, carrying yourself as if worry were someone else's job
examples: He strolled into the final exam twenty minutes late with an insouciance that enraged everyone who had studied.
She shrugged off the criticism with typical insouciance.
words.etymology: French, from in- (not) plus soucier (to worry), tracing back to Latin sollicitare, to disturb.

definitions: Cheerfully unconcerned, carrying yourself as if worry were someone else's job.
examples: The insouciant skier whistled through the avalanche briefing, then ordered a hot chocolate.
She gave an insouciant shrug when the deadline moved up.
words.etymology: From French in plus soucier, to worry, from Latin sollicitare: literally not troubling oneself.

definitions: Refusing to compromise or budge, holding a position past the point of reason.
examples: Both delegations arrived intransigent, and the summit produced nothing but a group photo.
He's intransigent about the thermostat setting.
words.etymology: From Spanish los intransigentes, the name of an uncompromising political faction, built on Latin in plus transigere, to come to an agreement.

definitions: To attack something in words, bitterly and at length; always followed by 'against.'
examples: The columnist has inveighed against the ring road for twenty years, and today they finally poured the concrete.
Grandpa inveighs against self-checkout machines at every family dinner.
words.etymology: From Latin invehi, to be carried into, hence to attack, from in- plus vehere, to carry: to ride in against a target.

definitions: to lure someone into doing something through charm, flattery, and gentle deception
examples: The con man inveigled three widows into 'investing' in a marina that existed only as a brochure.
Somehow she inveigled her way into the sold-out premiere.
words.etymology: From Anglo-French enveogler, based on French aveugle (blind): to blind someone into going along.

definitions: so long established in a habit or character that change is off the table
examples: An inveterate haggler, she bargained over the price of a museum ticket that was already free.
He's an inveterate early riser, vacation or not.
words.etymology: Latin inveterare, to make old, from vetus, old: a habit that has aged into the bone.

definitions: By that very fact, automatically; used when one thing entails another without any further step.
examples: He signed the rival's contract, and ipso facto ceased to be a member of the guild.
If you missed the deadline, you're ipso facto out of the raffle.
words.etymology: Latin for 'by the fact itself,' borrowed whole from legal and scholastic usage.

definitions: To wail in high, piercing grief, especially in lament for the dead.
examples: The wind keened through the rigging all night, and no one aboard slept.
Mourners keened at the graveside in the old style, a sound he never forgot.
words.etymology: From Irish caoinim, I wail or lament, borrowed from the Irish funeral tradition of sung mourning.

definitions: The slow toll of a bell for a death, and by extension a sign that something is ending, as in death knell.
examples: The knell carried from the village church across the frozen fields, and the men in the yard took off their caps.
The ruling sounded the death knell for the old tax loophole.
words.etymology: From Old English cnyll, the stroke of a bell, imitative of the sound.

definitions: A gap or missing piece, in a manuscript, an argument, or a body of knowledge.
examples: The archive has a maddening lacuna: every letter from the crucial winter of 1893 is missing.
There's a lacuna in your proposal where the budget should be.
words.etymology: From Latin lacuna, a pool or hollow, from lacus, lake: a hole where something has drained away.

definitions: A dreamy heaviness of body and mind, tiredness that feels almost pleasant.
examples: A tropical languor overtook the crew once the ship crossed into the doldrums.
She spent the holiday in a state of happy languor, book open and eyes closed.
words.etymology: From Latin languere, to be faint, the noun form of the weakness in languid.

definitions: a recurring theme that keeps surfacing through a work or a life, each return carrying accumulated meaning
examples: Locked doors are the novel's leitmotif, appearing in every chapter until the reader flinches at the word key.
Reinvention is the leitmotif of her whole career.
words.etymology: German Leitmotiv, leading motive, from Wagner criticism, where a musical phrase tags a character each time they appear.

definitions: Perfectly clear and transparent, whether water, light, or prose.
examples: The pool below the falls was so limpid that the trout seemed to hang in air.
Her limpid explanations made the hardest chapter feel obvious.
words.etymology: From Latin limpidus, clear, possibly related to lympha, clear water.

definitions: to treat someone as a celebrity, fussing over them as the star attraction
examples: After the memoir hit the lists, the once-ignored translator was lionized at festivals on three continents.
The press lionized the young quarterback before he'd started a full season.
words.etymology: From the lions once kept at the Tower of London, a must-see sight, so to lionize is to treat a person as the sight everyone must see.

definitions: Rich, dark soil balanced between sand and clay, the kind gardeners dream about.
examples: He crumbled the loam in his fist and smelled rain, roots, and forty good harvests.
The valley's deep loam grows the best potatoes in the county.
words.etymology: From Old English lam, clay or mud, related to lime in its old sense of sticky earth.

definitions: Mournful and gloomy in an exaggerated, almost theatrical way.
examples: The basset hound wore its usual lugubrious expression, as though the mail had brought bad news.
He read the minutes in a lugubrious drone that made the meeting feel like a funeral.
words.etymology: From Latin lugere, to mourn.

definitions: a secret scheme or plot, usually one of several, engineered to harm or manipulate
examples: The prince survived his uncle's machinations only because the poisoner switched sides.
Office machinations decided the promotion before the interviews began.
words.etymology: Latin machinari, to contrive, from machina (machine): plotting as engineering.
