definitions: A violent whirlpool, and by extension any chaotic, churning situation that sucks everything into it.
examples: The skiff was dragged toward the maelstrom where the two tides met over the reef.
She resigned rather than be pulled into the maelstrom of the merger.
words.etymology: From early Dutch malen, to grind, plus stroom, stream, the name given to a notorious whirlpool off Norway.

definitions: Notes written in the margins of a book; by extension, minor peripheral matter around a main subject.
examples: The used copy turned out to be a treasure: three generations of marginalia arguing with the text and with each other.
Her real opinions live in the marginalia of the meeting agenda.
words.etymology: From Latin margo, marginis, edge: the things belonging to the margin.

definitions: A stickler for rules and discipline who enforces every regulation to the letter, usually joylessly.
examples: The new principal was a martinet who timed hallway transitions with a stopwatch.
Our scoutmaster was a martinet about tent corners.
words.etymology: From Jean Martinet, a seventeenth-century French drillmaster whose brutal discipline shaped the army of Louis XIV.

definitions: Tearfully sentimental in a sloppy, excessive way, often with drink involved.
examples: By the third round of toasts the groom's uncle turned maudlin, weeping about a childhood dog no one else remembered.
The film's final act gets maudlin and loses its nerve.
words.etymology: From Mary Magdalene, whom medieval art always showed weeping; her name, worn down to maudlin, came to mean tearful excess.

definitions: afraid to speak plainly, softening everything into vague, evasive mush
examples: The mealy-mouthed press release regretted 'outcomes' without mentioning the recall or the injuries.
Don't be mealy-mouthed with me, just say you disagree.
words.etymology: From meal, ground grain: a mouth full of soft meal, unable to produce a hard-edged word.

definitions: So smooth and sweet-sounding that it seems to pour, usually said of voices and music.
examples: The radio host's mellifluous baritone carried the town through a thousand winter mornings.
The professor's mellifluous lectures made attendance feel optional and irresistible at once.
words.etymology: From Latin mel, honey, plus fluere, to flow: literally flowing with honey.

definitions: habitually lying, or built out of lies
examples: The report was mendacious from its first footnote, citing studies that did not exist.
He gave a mendacious account of where the money went.
words.etymology: Latin mendax, lying, possibly related to mendum (a fault or defect): speech with the defects built in.

definitions: Prone to sudden, unpredictable changes of mood or mind, quick and volatile like liquid metal.
examples: Working for the mercurial designer meant a promotion on Monday could become a shouting match by Thursday.
Her mercurial moods make it hard to know which version of her will show up.
words.etymology: From the god and planet Mercury: people born under Mercury were thought quick, lively, and changeable, like the darting metal named for him.

definitions: Flashy and superficially attractive but worthless underneath; appeal that has been bought rather than earned.
examples: The pitch deck had a meretricious glitter, all stock photography and borrowed statistics, and the investors saw through it by slide four.
The lobby's gold trim struck me as meretricious rather than grand.
words.etymology: From Latin meretrix, a prostitute, from merere, to earn: attractions offered for pay.

definitions: A heavy, unwholesome vapor or atmosphere, literal or moral, that hangs over a place and poisons it.
examples: A miasma rose off the flooded fields at dusk, and the villagers swore it carried fever.
A miasma of suspicion hung over the office after the audit.
words.etymology: From Greek miasma, pollution or defilement, from miainein, to stain; bad air was once blamed for disease.

definitions: A dreamy musical piece evoking night, or a painting of a night scene.
examples: Through the open window someone was practicing a Chopin nocturne, and the street seemed to slow to listen.
Whistler's nocturnes reduce the Thames at night to a few floating lights.
words.etymology: From French nocturne, from Latin nocturnus, of the night, from nox, night.

definitions: Existing as an idea or assumption rather than in reality; hypothetical, on paper only.
examples: The department's notional budget bore no relation to the money that actually arrived each quarter.
Our savings plan is still notional; nothing has moved to the account yet.
words.etymology: From Latin notio, an idea or concept, from noscere, to know.

definitions: To remove the need for something in advance; to head off a problem so it never has to be solved.
examples: A single clarifying sentence in the contract would have obviated the entire lawsuit.
Ordering groceries online obviates the Saturday store run.
words.etymology: From Latin obviare, to meet in the way and block, from ob viam, in the path.

definitions: Concerning what exists and what kinds of things there are; about the nature of being itself rather than our knowledge of it.
examples: Their dispute was ontological at bottom: she thought corporations were real entities, he thought they were convenient fictions.
Whether numbers exist is an ontological question, not a mathematical one.
words.etymology: From Greek on, ontos, being, plus logos, account: the study of what is.

definitions: harsh public scorn and disgrace, the collective contempt aimed at someone who has crossed a line
examples: After the doping report, the opprobrium was so total that sponsors deleted decade-old photos.
The plan drew opprobrium from both parties, a rare achievement.
words.etymology: Latin opprobrare, to reproach, from probrum (a disgrace): shame delivered at volume.

definitions: someone newly arrived at wealth or status who has not absorbed the manners of the class they joined, said with a sniff
examples: The old families dismissed the crypto millionaire as a parvenu who bought his library by the yard.
The board treated the young founder like a parvenu until his second company sold.
words.etymology: French, past participle of parvenir, to arrive, from Latin pervenire: one who has just arrived.

definitions: The soft film of age on a surface, like the green on old bronze, and the mellow character time gives things.
examples: The statue's patina had gone the exact green of the harbor it had watched for a century.
The oak table has a patina you cannot fake with stain.
words.etymology: From Italian patina, from Latin patina, a shallow dish, after the film that forms on old bronze dishware.

definitions: The soft half-shadow between full darkness and full light.
A surrounding fringe or borderland of something, where it shades off into ambiguity.
examples: She waited in the penumbra of the streetlamp, visible enough to be safe, dim enough to be forgotten.
The question falls in the penumbra of the law, where the statute gives no clear answer.
words.etymology: From Latin paene, almost, plus umbra, shadow: an almost-shadow.

definitions: Treacherous, betraying the trust of someone who had every reason to rely on you.
examples: The perfidious lieutenant sold the garrison's gate codes to the very army he had sworn to repel.
It was a perfidious move to leak your own friend's plans.
words.etymology: From Latin per plus fides, faith: perfidy is faith broken through and through.

definitions: moving from place to place rather than settling, a life conducted in transit
examples: Her peripatetic childhood, eleven schools in nine years, left her fluent in the art of the first impression.
He kept a peripatetic schedule, a different city every week.
words.etymology: Greek peripatein, to walk around, after Aristotle's habit of lecturing while strolling the Lyceum.

definitions: Sharp-eyed about what is really going on; able to see through surfaces to the truth of a situation.
examples: The most perspicacious question came from the newest hire, who asked why the flagship metric had been quietly redefined in March.
A perspicacious reader will notice the narrator never mentions his brother again.
words.etymology: From Latin perspicere, to see through, from per- plus specere, to look.

definitions: calm to the point of seeming unexcitable, temperamentally immune to panic
examples: While the newsroom erupted over the verdict, the phlegmatic editor finished his sandwich before rewriting the front page.
Our phlegmatic pilot announced the engine trouble like a weather update.
words.etymology: Greek phlegma, the cold moist humor of medieval medicine, thought to make its bearers slow and serene.

definitions: A flash of wounded-pride irritation, resentment at feeling slighted.
examples: In a fit of pique, the chess master swept the pieces off the board and demanded a rematch.
She skipped the reunion out of pique over the invitation's late arrival.
words.etymology: From French piquer, to prick or sting: pique is the prick your ego feels.

definitions: To speak evasively or misleadingly, skating around the truth without quite lying outright.
examples: Asked when he had learned of the leak, the director prevaricated so elaborately that the committee took a recess to diagram his answer.
Stop prevaricating and tell me what happened to the car.
words.etymology: From Latin praevaricari, literally to walk crookedly or straddle, said of an advocate who secretly colluded with the other side.

definitions: At first look, before deeper examination; describing evidence strong enough to stand unless rebutted.
examples: The fingerprints on the ledger made a prima facie case of embezzlement, whatever explanation might come later.
Prima facie, the numbers look fine; give me a day to dig.
words.etymology: Latin for 'at first face,' from primus and facies: how a matter appears on first appearance.

definitions: Proven, rock-solid honesty and integrity, the kind of uprightness a career can rest on.
examples: Forty years on the bench without a whisper of scandal made her probity the courthouse's quiet legend.
The auditors vouched for his probity.
words.etymology: From Latin probus, upright or good, the root also behind probe and prove.

definitions: using far too many words, long-winded to the point of tedium
examples: The judge ordered the prolix brief cut from ninety pages to twenty, and justice lost nothing.
His emails are so prolix that people skim to the last line.
words.etymology: Latin prolixus, poured out and flowing forth: speech that overflows its container.

definitions: scrupulously exact about fine points of conduct or procedure, correct down to the last detail
examples: The maître d' was punctilious about forks, realigning each one as though the table were a surgical tray.
She's punctilious about citations, every page number checked twice.
words.etymology: From Italian puntiglio, a fine point of honor, from Latin punctum, point.

definitions: Cowardly in a small-spirited way, lacking the nerve to act when courage is called for.
examples: The board's pusillanimous silence let the founder bully out the last honest engineer.
It was pusillanimous of me not to speak up at the meeting.
words.etymology: From Latin pusillus, very small, plus animus, spirit: literally tiny-souled, the exact opposite of magnanimous.

definitions: Generally assumed or reputed to be the case, with the strong hint that the assumption may not survive scrutiny.
examples: The putative author of the pamphlet turned out to be three people, none of them the man on the title page.
The putative benefits of the merger never showed up in the numbers.
words.etymology: From Latin putare, to think or reckon: the putative fact is the reckoned-to-be-true one.

definitions: Complaining in a whiny, fretful tone, finding fault as a constant background hum.
examples: The querulous passenger spent the whole flight cataloging grievances, from the seat angle to the color of the juice.
He gets querulous when he's tired, so we fed him and moved on.
words.etymology: From Latin queri, to complain, the same root as quarrel.

definitions: belonging to the ordinary everyday, routine to the point of invisibility
examples: After the expedition, he found the quotidian rituals of commuting and coffee lines almost unbearably strange.
The film finds drama in quotidian chores, laundry, dishes, school runs.
words.etymology: Latin quotidie, every day, from quot (how many) plus dies (day).

definitions: Stubbornly resistant to authority or direction, digging in against being managed.
examples: The recalcitrant witness answered every question with a shrug and a request for coffee.
The recalcitrant printer refuses every driver update.
words.etymology: From Latin recalcitrare, to kick back with the heels, from calx, heel: the image is a mule kicking at its handler.

definitions: Dealing with knowledge so specialized or obscure that few people have ever gone looking for it.
examples: Her dissertation topic was magnificently recondite: marginal punctuation in twelfth-century Bolognese legal manuscripts.
He collects recondite facts about canal engineering the way others collect stamps.
words.etymology: From Latin recondere, to hide away or store, from re- plus condere: recondite learning is knowledge kept in the back room.

definitions: Strict moral correctness, living by the straight edge of principle.
examples: The deacon's famous rectitude made the embezzlement charge land like a thunderclap.
She handled the refund with typical rectitude, down to the last cent.
words.etymology: From Latin rectus, straight: moral straightness, as opposed to crookedness.

definitions: Smelling strongly of something.
Evocative, so saturated with associations that it summons a time or place.
examples: The stairwell was redolent of woodsmoke and wet wool, the smell of every winter of her childhood.
The kitchen was redolent with garlic by the time the guests arrived.
words.etymology: From Latin redolere, to give off a smell, from olere, to smell.

definitions: commanding respect tinged with fear, an opponent or figure you underestimate at your peril
examples: The redoubtable head librarian had outlasted six directors and two attempts to digitize her card catalog.
Their legal team is redoubtable, settle if you can.
words.etymology: From French redouter, to dread, ultimately from Latin dubitare, to doubt: someone who makes you doubt yourself.

definitions: To mistake an idea for an object: you take something abstract, like 'the economy', and start treating it as a solid thing with a will of its own.
examples: The danger of dashboards is that they reify 'engagement' until managers believe the number is the customer.
Don't reify 'the market'; it's just millions of people making choices.
words.etymology: From Latin res, thing, plus facere, to make: to thing-ify.

definitions: To protest or object earnestly, trying to reason someone out of a course of action.
examples: The engineers remonstrated with management for weeks about the launch date, and were thanked, and ignored.
She remonstrated with the referee, politely and pointlessly.
words.etymology: From Latin re- plus monstrare, to show: to point something out again and again in protest.

definitions: quick, witty back-and-forth conversation, the verbal equivalent of a rally at the net
examples: The two anchors' repartee was the only unscripted thing on the broadcast, and the best.
Their dinner-party repartee left the rest of us happily spectating.
words.etymology: French repartie, a swift reply, from repartir (to set off again, retort): conversation that keeps leaving the ground.

definitions: a swift, sharp comeback that turns an attack back on the attacker
examples: Heckled about his height, the candidate's riposte got a bigger laugh than the heckler did.
She always thinks of the perfect riposte an hour too late.
words.etymology: French riposte, from Italian risposta (reply): fencing's counter-thrust borrowed for conversation.

definitions: To churn a liquid into cloudy turbulence.
To stir up and unsettle people or situations.
examples: The storm roiled the bay until it ran brown with dredged-up silt.
Rumors of layoffs roiled the office all week.
words.etymology: Of uncertain origin, appearing in English in the 1500s for stirring sediment up into water.

definitions: Having an unhealthy yellowish tinge, said of skin and complexions.
examples: Months underground had left the miners sallow, blinking in the pithead light.
The fluorescent lighting makes everyone look sallow in that office.
words.etymology: From Old English salo, dusky or dirty yellow.

definitions: Cool composure under pressure, staying unflustered when everyone else is panicking.
examples: With absolute sangfroid, the pilot narrated the engine failure like a weather update.
She defused the client blowup with impressive sangfroid.
words.etymology: From French sang froid, literally cold blood: calmness as low temperature.

definitions: a long, angry, usually tedious piece of writing or speech pushing a grievance
examples: He posted a two-thousand-word screed about the homeowners association and its flag policy.
Her resignation letter turned into a screed against middle management.
words.etymology: Old English screade, a shred or torn strip: a long strip of material, then a long strip of complaint.

definitions: Suggesting a tomb: gloomy, hollow, and deathly quiet.
examples: The abandoned station had a sepulchral hush broken only by water dripping somewhere in the dark.
He announced the quarterly results in a sepulchral tone that told everyone what was coming.
words.etymology: From Latin sepulcrum, tomb, from sepelire, to bury.

definitions: a word, phrase, or belief that marks who belongs to a group and who does not
an old slogan or doctrine a group clings to after it has stopped meaning much
examples: Pronouncing the town's name correctly was the shibboleth that separated locals from journalists.
Free trade had become a party shibboleth no one dared question.
words.etymology: Hebrew shibboleth, an ear of grain: in the Book of Judges, fleeing Ephraimites who pronounced it 'sibboleth' were caught at the river ford and killed.

definitions: the one absolutely essential ingredient, the thing without which the whole enterprise cannot exist
examples: Trust is the sine qua non of a good kitchen, without it the line falls apart on the first busy night.
A working demo is the sine qua non of any pitch to this fund.
words.etymology: Latin, without which not, from the legal phrase causa sine qua non, the cause without which the result could not occur.

definitions: The position that only your own mind is certain to exist; more loosely, a self-absorption so total that other people barely register.
examples: The memoir slides from honesty into solipsism, as though the author's divorce were the only event of the decade.
Teenage solipsism is assuming everyone at the party is thinking about your haircut.
words.etymology: From Latin solus, alone, plus ipse, self: the doctrine of the self alone.

definitions: Drowsy and half asleep, or so quiet and slow that it makes you feel that way.
examples: The somnolent village dozed through the siesta hours, even its fountains running slow.
The somnolent hum of the air conditioner nearly finished off the afternoon class.
words.etymology: From Latin somnus, sleep, the root shared by insomnia.

definitions: Clever-sounding argument designed to deceive rather than to find truth; reasoning as a con game.
examples: The defense's case was elegant sophistry, each step plausible and the whole thing rotten.
Calling a pay cut a 'compensation realignment' is just sophistry.
words.etymology: From the Sophists, the paid rhetoric teachers of classical Athens whose name Plato turned into an insult for hire-a-argument cleverness.

definitions: To spread gradually through or over something, the way blush spreads through a face or dawn through a sky.
examples: First light suffused the snowfield with rose and gold before the sun itself appeared.
A quiet pride suffused her voice as she described her students.
words.etymology: From Latin sub, under, plus fundere, to pour: poured in from beneath.

definitions: in a category of its own, so unlike anything else that comparisons fail
examples: Critics gave up trying to classify the album, a sui generis blend of Appalachian banjo and Tokyo club music.
Her teaching style is sui generis, no workshop produced it.
words.etymology: Latin, of its own kind, a term from law and taxonomy for things that fit no existing class.

definitions: Coolly condescending, looking down on others with an air of bored superiority.
examples: The supercilious sommelier repeated our wine order back to us slowly, as if translating for children.
She gave the tourists a supercilious once-over and turned away.
words.etymology: From Latin supercilium, eyebrow: contempt delivered with a raised brow.

definitions: an excess of something, especially so much of a good thing that it turns unpleasant
examples: By the third wedding of the summer, she had developed a surfeit of champagne toasts and chicken entrées.
There's a surfeit of streaming shows and no time to watch them.
words.etymology: Old French surfaire, to overdo, from sur (over) plus faire (to do).

definitions: Of the woods: shaded, leafy, and belonging to forest places.
examples: They were married in a sylvan clearing, light falling in shafts through the oaks.
The cabin offers a sylvan setting twenty minutes from town.
words.etymology: From Latin silva, forest, the same root behind Pennsylvania, Penn's woods.

definitions: Pushing a particular agenda while pretending to be neutral; slanted writing or argument with its thumb on the scale.
examples: The documentary was so tendentious that even viewers who agreed with its conclusion felt handled rather than informed.
His summary of the meeting was tendentious; he left out everyone who disagreed with him.
words.etymology: From Latin tendere, to stretch or aim, via tendency: writing that leans toward its predetermined end.

definitions: a person who flatters and grovels before someone powerful to stay in their good graces
examples: Every tyrant in the building had a toady, and Mr. Pell had three.
He's the boss's toady, so watch what you say at lunch.
words.etymology: Short for toadeater, the quack's assistant who swallowed a supposedly poisonous toad so the mountebank could sell the cure.

definitions: aggressively defiant and eager for a fight, hostile before anyone has given cause
examples: The truculent defendant argued with his own attorney, the judge, and finally the court stenographer.
He gets truculent whenever anyone questions his numbers.
words.etymology: Latin trux, fierce or savage, via truculentus.

definitions: Cloudy and murky with stirred-up sediment, the opposite of clear.
Muddled and confused, as thinking can be.
examples: The river ran turbid and brown for a week after the floods, carrying half a hillside to the sea.
His turbid argument lost the jury within minutes.
words.etymology: From Latin turbidus, disturbed, from turba, turmoil or a churning crowd.

definitions: Shameful wickedness or depravity; the legal phrase moral turpitude covers conduct vile enough to disqualify someone.
examples: The tabloid promised revelations of turpitude but delivered only a parking ticket and an awkward karaoke video.
He was disbarred for acts of moral turpitude.
words.etymology: From Latin turpis, base, ugly, or shameful.

definitions: Offense taken at a slight, real or imagined; almost always in the phrase to take umbrage.
examples: The novelist took umbrage at being introduced as a hobbyist and left before the salad course.
He takes umbrage at the mildest editing notes.
words.etymology: From Latin umbra, shadow: to take umbrage is to feel a shadow fall over you.

definitions: oily in manner, so smoothly flattering and earnest that it feels greasy
examples: The funeral director's unctuous sympathy made the family trust him less with every syllable.
He gave the investors an unctuous welcome and inflated numbers.
words.etymology: Latin unctum, ointment, from ungere (to anoint): a personality you could fry with.

definitions: Corruptible, willing to sell your influence or principles for money.
examples: The venal inspector waved through the cracked foundation for an envelope of cash.
The report described a venal licensing office where every stamp had a price.
words.etymology: From Latin venum, that which is for sale: a venal official is literally purchasable.

definitions: The appearance of being true or real; the quality in fiction or testimony that makes you believe it, whether or not it is accurate.
examples: The forger's genius was verisimilitude: coffee rings, a torn corner, an address that existed until 1934.
The dialogue lacks verisimilitude; no two teenagers have ever spoken like that.
words.etymology: From Latin verus, true, plus similis, like: likeness to truth.

definitions: To corrupt or spoil something so that it loses its force or validity; a flaw that poisons the whole.
examples: The discovery that the samples had thawed overnight vitiated three years of trial data.
One coerced signature can vitiate an entire contract.
words.etymology: From Latin vitium, a fault or vice: to vitiate is to infect a thing with a defect.

definitions: Glassy: having the look, texture, or nature of glass.
examples: Cold had turned the pond vitreous, a black pane over slow gold leaves.
The pottery is fired until the glaze turns vitreous and waterproof.
words.etymology: From Latin vitrum, glass.

definitions: speaking in fluent, rolling, unstoppable streams of words
examples: Normally shy, she became voluble on the subject of orbital mechanics, and the party gathered around her.
He's a voluble host who barely lets his guests finish a sentence.
words.etymology: Latin volvere, to roll: words that roll out in an easy, continuous turn.

definitions: Pale and weak-looking, like winter light or a tired face.
examples: A wan sun struggled through the overcast, giving light without any warmth.
She managed a wan smile from the hospital bed.
words.etymology: From Old English wann, meaning dark or gloomy; over centuries the word flipped from darkness to bloodless pallor.

definitions: A ghost or ghostly apparition, especially a thin, insubstantial one, and by extension anything pale and barely there.
examples: A wraith of mist detached itself from the river and drifted across the towpath.
After the flu he wandered the house like a wraith for a week.
words.etymology: A Scots word of unknown origin, entering English through 16th century Scottish accounts of apparitions.

definitions: A soft, gentle breeze, the kindest wind there is.
examples: A zephyr off the water stirred the awnings and carried the smell of jasmine up the hill.
The forecast promised zephyrs, not the gale we actually got.
words.etymology: From Zephyros, the Greek god of the mild west wind, gentlest of the wind brothers.

definitions: To sketch something in rough outline, or to foreshadow it faintly, giving a shadow of the full picture.
examples: The introduction adumbrates an argument the book never quite delivers, like a trailer for a film that was never shot.
Her early notebooks adumbrate the theory she would publish twenty years later.
words.etymology: From Latin adumbrare, to sketch in shadow, from ad- plus umbra, shadow.

definitions: political propaganda pushed through art, theater, or media, agitation dressed as culture
examples: The mural was pure agitprop, tractors and sunrises and not one tired face.
Critics dismissed the film as agitprop for the ruling party.
words.etymology: Russian blend of agitatsiya and propaganda, from the Soviet Communist Party's Department of Agitation and Propaganda.

definitions: The rosy light that flushes mountain peaks just before sunrise or after sunset.
examples: They reached the pass as alpenglow set the summits burning pink above a valley already in night.
The lodge deck faces west for the alpenglow, so book dinner at dusk.
words.etymology: A loan-translation of German Alpengluehen, literally Alps-glow.

definitions: Someone whose job is writing down another person's words: the trusted hand that takes dictation while the author paces and talks.
examples: Blind in his last decade, the historian composed every chapter aloud to a patient amanuensis.
She jokes that she's her husband's amanuensis, since he dictates all his texts while driving.
words.etymology: From Latin servus a manu, the slave at hand, the secretary who wrote at the master's hand.

definitions: The unmoored, rootless malaise of people whose society's norms have broken down, leaving no shared rules to live by.
examples: The novel captures the anomie of a factory town after the plant closes: nobody knows what a good life looks like anymore.
Sociologists blamed the spike in dropouts partly on suburban anomie.
words.etymology: From Greek a plus nomos, without law; the sociologist Emile Durkheim made it a term of art for normlessness.

definitions: a short, blunt, instructive saying, a maxim with its sleeves rolled up
examples: The foreman ran the yard on apothegms: measure twice, blame once.
Franklin's almanac dispensed apothegms about thrift and early rising.
words.etymology: Greek apophthegma, something spoken out plainly, from apo- (forth) plus phthengesthai (to utter).

definitions: playfully sly and knowing, teasing with one eyebrow raised
examples: Her arch reply, 'How brave of you to say so publicly,' took a moment to detonate.
He gave me an arch look when I claimed I'd read the whole contract.
words.etymology: From the prefix arch- (chief, as in arch-villain): compounds like arch-rogue rubbed their mischief off onto the bare word.

definitions: At a slanting angle, obliquely rather than straight on.
examples: Late sun came aslant through the orchard, laying long bars of gold between the trees.
Rain drove aslant across the headlights all the way home.
words.etymology: From Middle English, built from a-, on, plus slant, from a Scandinavian source meaning to slope.

definitions: To declare something solemnly and emphatically, staking your earnestness on its truth.
examples: The witness asseverated, hand flat on the rail, that he had never once opened the safe.
She asseverated that the casserole contained no meat, and the vegetarians ate in peace.
words.etymology: From Latin asseverare, to assert in earnest, from ad- plus severus, serious.

definitions: Apart, into separate pieces, usually with violence, as in torn asunder.
examples: Lightning split the ancient elm asunder, and the two halves fell away from each other like opening doors.
The scandal tore the coalition asunder within a month.
words.etymology: From Old English on sundran, into separate places, from sundor, apart, kin to sunder.

definitions: Golden or gilded.
Ornate and flowery in style, gold-plated language.
examples: An aureate haze hung over the stubble fields in the last hour before dark.
The ambassador's aureate speech said almost nothing at great length.
words.etymology: From Latin aureus, golden, from aurum, gold, the Au of the periodic table.

definitions: light, playful, witty conversation, banter with a French accent
examples: Their courtship was conducted entirely in badinage; the first serious sentence either spoke was the proposal.
The hosts' badinage filled the time between segments.
words.etymology: French badiner, to jest, from badin, a joker, ultimately from a Provencal word meaning to gape.

definitions: Sour-tempered and full of spleen, as if bad digestion had become a personality.
Of colors, sickly and nauseating.
examples: The bilious columnist greeted every new building in the city with a fresh volley of contempt.
He was in a bilious mood after the flight delay.
words.etymology: From Latin bilis, bile: humor theory blamed excess bile for foul moods.

definitions: To coax someone with smooth flattery, working on them with pleasing words.
examples: The courtiers blandished the young king with talk of his genius until he believed the harvest would fix itself.
No amount of blandishing will get me on that roller coaster.
words.etymology: From Latin blandiri, to flatter, from blandus, smooth or caressing, the same root as bland.

definitions: to speak at windy, self-important length while saying almost nothing
examples: Given a microphone and no time limit, the chairman bloviated through the lunch break and into legend.
Cable panels exist so people can bloviate on schedule.
words.etymology: Mock-Latin American slang inflating the word 'blow,' famously attached to President Warren G. Harding, a champion practitioner.

definitions: a clever, quotable remark, the little jewel of a conversation
examples: She collected the ambassador's bon mots in a notebook, suspecting rightly that a staffer wrote them.
He delivered one good bon mot per party and left before it wore off.
words.etymology: French, literally 'good word': wit measured out one perfect phrase at a time.

definitions: swaggering, empty boasting, the performance of confidence
examples: Beneath the press-conference braggadocio, the challenger's hands would not stay still.
His braggadocio about the deal evaporated when the numbers came out.
words.etymology: From Braggadocchio, the boastful phony knight in Spenser's Faerie Queene, English brag wearing an Italian ending.

definitions: Quick to find fault and fond of raising trick objections; criticism designed to trap rather than improve.
examples: The referee's report was captious from the first line, objecting even to the paper's use of standard notation.
Don't be captious; the essay's argument is sound even if a comma is missing.
words.etymology: From Latin captiosus, ensnaring or fallacious, from capere, to seize: objections built to catch you.

definitions: To raise trivial, fussy objections; to quibble about details that do not matter.
examples: The committee approved the design in ten minutes and then caviled for an hour about the font on the cover page.
I won't cavil over who owes the extra dollar.
words.etymology: From Latin cavillari, to jeer or banter, from cavilla, a jesting taunt.

definitions: The dramatic play of light against deep shadow, in painting or any scene lit that way.
examples: A single lamp made a chiaroscuro of the interrogation room, half his face in gold, half gone.
The photographer shoots in harsh chiaroscuro, all noir shadows and blown highlights.
words.etymology: Italian, from chiaro, light or clear, plus oscuro, dark, the Renaissance painters' term for light-dark contrast.

definitions: To prove an argument or person decisively wrong; refutation with finality.
examples: A single photograph from the harbor confuted the entire official chronology.
Her experiment confuted a theory that had stood for sixty years.
words.etymology: From Latin confutare, to check or silence: to put an argument down for good.

definitions: Stubbornly, insolently defiant of authority, especially of a court's orders.
examples: The contumacious defendant addressed the judge as 'buddy' and was jailed for contempt by lunch.
The board declared the branch office contumacious after it ignored a third directive.
words.etymology: From Latin contumacia, stubbornness, possibly related to tumere, to swell: defiance as puffed-up pride.

definitions: Of twilight: dim, in-between, belonging to dusk or dawn.
Active at twilight, as deer and moths are.
examples: The photographer loved the crepuscular hour when the streetlights and the sky held equal weight.
Rabbits are crepuscular, so walk at dawn or dusk if you want to see them.
words.etymology: From Latin crepusculum, twilight.

definitions: To talk or write at length on a theme, embroidering it with variations; from the musical sense of a melody sung above the main tune.
examples: Give him one glass of port and he will descant for an hour on the decline of letter writing.
The guide descanted on medieval guilds while we shivered in the doorway.
words.etymology: From Latin dis- plus cantus, song: originally a melody woven above the plainchant, later talk that embellishes a theme.

definitions: Something needed and wanted but still missing; the acknowledged gap on everyone's wish list.
examples: A reliable dating method for the site remains the great desideratum of the excavation, forty years in.
A quiet dishwasher was the last desideratum of the renovation.
words.etymology: From Latin desiderare, to long for: the thing longed for, its plural desiderata.

definitions: A long, elaborate, formal examination of a subject, in speech or writing; often longer than anyone wanted.
examples: Asked whether the bridge was safe, the consultant delivered a forty-minute disquisition on the history of load modeling.
One question about wine got us a full disquisition on Burgundy soil.
words.etymology: From Latin disquirere, to investigate thoroughly, from dis- plus quaerere, to seek.

definitions: to hide what you really think or feel behind a constructed appearance
examples: Trained to dissimulate, the double agent grieved his handler's death only in locked rooms.
She couldn't dissimulate her boredom through the third hour of speeches.
words.etymology: Latin dissimulare, from dis- plus simulare (to feign): to feign away, making the real thing invisible.

definitions: Heavy with sorrow or expressing grief and pain.
examples: A dolorous wind moaned in the chimneys of the shuttered hotel all that off-season.
The ballad's dolorous refrain stuck with her for days.
words.etymology: From Latin dolor, pain or grief, the same root as condolence.

definitions: A state of indignant, huffy resentment; almost always heard in the phrase in high dudgeon.
examples: She swept out of the board meeting in high dudgeon, taking the good projector remote with her.
He's been in a dudgeon since we vetoed his logo.
words.etymology: In use since the 1500s with no established origin; the trail simply goes cold.

definitions: Radiantly, almost overwhelmingly bright, pouring out light.
examples: The monstrance caught the noon sun and went effulgent, too bright to look at directly.
She came down the stairs with an effulgent grin that answered the question before anyone asked.
words.etymology: From Latin ex, out, plus fulgere, to shine: shining outward.

definitions: the hidden power behind an official leader, the adviser who actually pulls the strings
examples: The chief of staff was the administration's éminence grise, unelected, unphotographed, and obeyed.
Every studio has an éminence grise whose notes decide which films get made.
words.etymology: French for gray eminence, the nickname of Père Joseph, the gray-robed friar who advised Cardinal Richelieu from the shadows.

definitions: A formal expression of glowing praise for a person or achievement.
examples: The festschrift opened with an encomium so lavish that the honoree, seated in the front row, visibly considered the exits.
The retirement dinner featured one encomium after another.
words.etymology: From Greek enkomion, the victory song sung in the komos, the revel procession for a triumphant athlete.

definitions: An inferior imitator of a great original; a follower who inherits the style but not the genius.
examples: After the master's death the studio filled with epigones, each painting his shadows without his light.
Every hit show spawns a season of epigones.
words.etymology: From Greek epigonoi, the after-born, the name given to the lesser sons of the Seven against Thebes.

definitions: to speak or write at generous length on a topic, roaming across it in full detail
examples: Invited to say a few words, the professor expatiated on medieval salt routes until the caterers began stacking chairs.
Give him an opening and he'll expatiate on vintage synthesizers all night.
words.etymology: Latin exspatiari, to wander from the path, from ex plus spatium, space: talk that strolls wherever it likes.

definitions: To argue earnestly with someone against a course of action, reasoning and protesting at once.
examples: The old foreman expostulated with the owner on the factory steps, hat in hand, arithmetic on his lips.
I expostulated with my brother about the motorcycle, and he bought it anyway.
words.etymology: From Latin expostulare, to demand urgently, from ex- plus postulare, to demand.

definitions: without preparation or notes, composed on the spot
examples: When the teleprompter died, she spoke extempore for twenty minutes and gave the best address of the campaign.
He prefers to toast extempore rather than read from a card.
words.etymology: Latin ex tempore, 'out of the moment': speech drawn from the instant itself.

definitions: Artificially manufactured rather than arising naturally; a contrived product passed off as genuine.
examples: The outrage was factitious, assembled overnight by a public relations firm and three rented pundits.
The show's suspense feels factitious, engineered by editing rather than events.
words.etymology: From Latin facticius, made by art, from facere, to make: the same root as fact, but for things merely made up.

definitions: an unhurried wanderer of city streets who strolls to observe rather than to arrive
examples: He spent his Paris years as a flâneur, logging arcades and café faces in a notebook no one ever saw.
Give me one free afternoon in a new city and I'm a happy flâneur.
words.etymology: French, from flâner, to stroll idly, elevated into a literary type by Baudelaire's essays on the man of the crowd.

definitions: Icy cold, frozen through, whether air, water, or a stare.
examples: They waded the gelid ford at first light, boots held overhead, feet gone numb in seconds.
The negotiator was met with a gelid silence.
words.etymology: From Latin gelu, frost, the root that also gives gelatin its set-when-cold nature.

definitions: a mocking, taunting remark thrown to wound or provoke
examples: He absorbed the crowd's gibes for nine innings and answered with a tenth-inning home run.
Their trash talk stayed friendly, gibes without malice.
words.etymology: Origin uncertain, possibly from Old French giber, to handle roughly: rough handling shrunk to words.

definitions: Twilight, the dim blue hour after sunset when shapes soften and lights come on.
examples: In the gloaming the loch turned to pewter and the first bats came out over the water.
The kids played one last game of tag in the gloaming before being called in.
words.etymology: From Old English glomung, twilight, related to glom and distantly to glow, the last of the light.

definitions: Speaking in short, dense maxims that sound wise but resist interpretation; cryptically aphoristic.
examples: The master's teaching was entirely gnomic, single sentences the students spent semesters unpacking.
His code reviews are gnomic: one line, no explanation, usually correct.
words.etymology: From Greek gnome, a maxim or judgment, from gignoskein, to know.

definitions: A bossy, belligerent, scolding old woman; a term as insulting as it is vivid.
examples: The boarding-house harridan patrolled the corridors at ten sharp, rattling doorknobs and snapping off lights.
Critics called the character a stock harridan with no inner life.
words.etymology: Possibly from French haridelle, a worn-out old horse; the insult rode into English in the 1600s.

definitions: The feathery white ice crystals that grow on surfaces during cold, clear, humid nights.
examples: Hoarfrost furred every fence wire and seed head, and the whole field glittered like broken glass.
Scrape gently; that's hoarfrost on the windshield, not ice.
words.etymology: From hoar, Old English har, gray with age, plus frost: frost that whitens the world like an old man's beard.

definitions: Designed to urge or exhort; language that pushes its audience toward action rather than informing it.
examples: The final chapter abandons analysis for hortatory mode, all imperatives and italics.
The coach's halftime speech was purely hortatory: no tactics, just fire.
words.etymology: From Latin hortari, to urge or encourage, the root of exhort.

definitions: Aiming at peace and reconciliation, deliberately conciliatory in tone.
examples: Her irenic opening speech disarmed delegations that had arrived spoiling for a fight.
He posted an irenic reply in a thread full of insults.
words.etymology: From Greek eirene, peace, the same root as the name Irene.

definitions: Thin and unsatisfying in substance, dull or naive in a way that leaves the mind hungry.
examples: The keynote was jejune, forty minutes of platitudes about synergy and dreams.
His analysis of the war was embarrassingly jejune.
words.etymology: From Latin jejunus, fasting or empty of food: writing that leaves you starved.

definitions: A long, bitter lament about the state of the world, prophesying doom and cataloging decline.
examples: The retiring dean's farewell address became a jeremiad against administrators, rankings, and the death of the seminar.
Every generation writes its jeremiad about the one coming up behind it.
words.etymology: From the prophet Jeremiah, whose biblical laments over a fallen Jerusalem set the template.

definitions: Given to weeping, or so sad it invites tears; tearful by disposition or design.
examples: The lachrymose ballad reduced the entire wedding party to smeared mascara by the second chorus.
He turns lachrymose at graduations, even for strangers' kids.
words.etymology: From Latin lacrima, tear.

definitions: Glowing softly, with light that seems to play or flicker over a surface rather than blaze.
examples: A lambent glow hung over the marsh where the fireflies rose and fell in the reeds.
The dying campfire threw a lambent light across their faces.
words.etymology: From Latin lambere, to lick, for the way low flame licks along a surface.

definitions: sleight of hand
clever trickery and misdirection, especially with numbers or words
examples: Through accounting legerdemain, the losses migrated to a subsidiary no regulator had ever heard of.
The magician's legerdemain was flawless even from the front row.
words.etymology: French leger de main, 'light of hand': the conjurer's quick fingers, hired out to metaphor.

definitions: To outline or depict, especially in light, as when a rim of brightness traces an edge.
To describe something in words with a portraitist's care.
examples: The eclipse limned the mountain ridge in a thread of silver fire.
The essay limns the mayor in a few devastating paragraphs.
words.etymology: From Middle English luminen, to illuminate a manuscript, from Latin luminare, to light up.

definitions: an uncontrollable flood of words, talkativeness as a pathology
examples: The deposition revealed his logorrhea; asked his name, he answered for six pages.
Her emails suffer from logorrhea, five hundred words to reschedule a call.
words.etymology: Greek logos (word) plus rhein (to flow), coined on the model of medical terms for things that flow when they shouldn't.

definitions: disreputable in an oddly attractive way, shady with a whiff of glamour
examples: They drank in a louche little bar behind the opera house where the bartender never asked names.
He had a louche charm that worried her parents.
words.etymology: French for cross-eyed or shady, from Latin luscus, one-eyed: something you cannot quite look at straight.

definitions: Sentimental in a sickly, cloying way that provokes cringe rather than tears.
examples: The memorial video turned mawkish when the soft piano started over baby photos of a fifty-year-old accountant.
The card's mawkish verse made her laugh instead of cry.
words.etymology: From Middle English mawke, maggot: it first meant nauseating, then nauseatingly sweet.

definitions: A person's bearing and expression, the overall air their face and posture give off.
examples: The ambassador's grave mien silenced the room before she said a word.
He has the mien of someone who has already read your resume and is unimpressed.
words.etymology: Probably shortened from demean, as in demeanor, with its spelling influenced by French mine, meaning look or appearance.

definitions: To rot slowly away, crumbling into dust and decay through neglect.
examples: Regimental flags moldered in the crypt, their battle honors dissolving thread by thread.
The files moldered in a basement for decades before anyone thought to digitize them.
words.etymology: Probably from mold in its old sense of loose earth, Old English molde, soil: to become soil again.

definitions: bitingly sarcastic, humor with teeth in it
examples: Her mordant caption under the official portrait cost her the job and made her famous.
He has a mordant wit that lands hardest on himself.
words.etymology: French mordre, to bite, from Latin mordere: wit that leaves tooth marks.

definitions: a flamboyant swindler who sells fake cures or promises from a public stage, literal or otherwise
examples: The wellness guru was a mountebank of the classic type, right down to the miracle tonic and the planted testimonials.
Investors eventually saw the founder for the mountebank he was.
words.etymology: Italian monta in banco, 'climbs on the bench': the quack literally mounted a bench in the piazza to hawk his cures.

definitions: the highest point achievable, the example beyond which nothing better exists
examples: For a generation of engineers, the Concorde remained the ne plus ultra of civil aviation.
That tasting menu is the ne plus ultra of the city's dining scene.
words.etymology: Latin, no more beyond, the warning legend said to be inscribed on the Pillars of Hercules at the edge of the known world.

definitions: Of no value or force; trifling, or legally void.
examples: Without an enforcement clause the treaty was nugatory, a press release with signatures.
The savings from skipping coffee are nugatory next to the rent.
words.etymology: From Latin nugae, trifles or jokes: nugatory things amount to trifles.

definitions: a storm of public abuse and blame, and the disgrace of standing in it
examples: He endured a decade of obloquy before the archives proved he had been the one telling the truth.
The whistleblower traded her career for obloquy and a clear conscience.
words.etymology: Latin obloqui, to speak against (ob- plus loqui): the crowd talking against you, all at once.

definitions: The deliberate withholding or muddying of knowledge, whether by opposing education or by writing in needless fog.
examples: The regime's obscurantism extended to banning maps that showed the neighboring country at all.
Reviewers accused the theorist of obscurantism, dressing thin ideas in impenetrable prose.
words.etymology: From Latin obscurare, to darken: the program of those who prefer the lights off.

definitions: Literally oily; figuratively, flattering and false with an unctuous, slippery charm.
examples: The oleaginous lobbyist greeted each senator as his oldest and dearest friend.
His oleaginous compliments slid right off her.
words.etymology: From Latin oleum, oil: the figurative sliminess is the oil transferred to manner.

definitions: pronounced with the mysterious authority of an oracle, solemn, ambiguous, and hard to question
examples: The chairman's oracular one-liners left each division convinced it had been promised the budget.
The coach gave an oracular answer about the starting lineup.
words.etymology: Latin oraculum, a divine pronouncement, from orare (to speak, to pray): the god's answer, famously open to interpretation.

definitions: full, round, and booming in voice
pompously grandiose in style
examples: The orotund narrator made even the cereal commercial sound like scripture.
His orotund prose buries a simple idea under organ music.
words.etymology: From Horace's Latin phrase ore rotundo, 'with rounded mouth,' his recipe for well-turned Greek eloquence.

definitions: Serving no practical purpose; functionally idle, said of words, arguments, or provisions that could vanish without loss.
examples: Half the report's recommendations were otiose, restating policies the company had adopted years earlier.
Cut the otiose adjectives and the paragraph sings.
words.etymology: From Latin otium, leisure: the otiose thing is at leisure, doing no work.

definitions: strikingly outside the bounds of convention, weird on purpose and past what taste usually allows
examples: Even the fashion press found the collection outré, hats shaped like fire hydrants, gowns of shredded receipts.
His jokes were a bit outré for a retirement lunch.
words.etymology: French, past participle of outrer, to push beyond, from Latin ultra: pushed past the limit.

definitions: prolonged, fussy, usually pointless talk or bother
historically, a parley or negotiation
examples: After an hour of palaver about seating charts, the actual vote took ninety seconds.
Getting a refund involved endless palaver with three departments.
words.etymology: Portuguese palavra, word: sailors' pidgin for the long trade negotiations on the West African coast, worn down to 'fuss.'

definitions: A manuscript scraped clean and written over, with the old text ghosting through; by extension anything layered with traces of its own past versions.
examples: Under ultraviolet light the prayer book revealed itself as a palimpsest, a lost treatise on floating bodies sleeping beneath the psalms.
The city is a palimpsest; every renovation exposes an older storefront.
words.etymology: From Greek palin, again, plus psestos, scraped: parchment scraped clean to be written on again.

definitions: A speech or text of elaborate, public, often official praise; eulogy at full ceremonial volume.
examples: The court poet produced the required panegyric, two hundred lines comparing the emperor favorably to the sun.
His toast crossed the line from warm words into outright panegyric.
words.etymology: From Greek panegyris, an all-gathering or festival assembly, where such speeches of praise were delivered.

definitions: Transparently clear, letting light pass straight through, said of water, air, and flawless prose.
examples: The bay was so pellucid that the anchored boats seemed to float on their own shadows.
Her pellucid summary settled an argument the committee had chewed on for weeks.
words.etymology: From Latin pellucidus, from per, through, plus lucere, to shine.

definitions: to walk about a place at an easy pace, especially in a leisurely inspecting way
examples: Each evening the colonel perambulated the garden walls, pausing to frown at the roses as if they were recruits.
We perambulated the old town until the cafés closed.
words.etymology: Latin per (through) plus ambulare (to walk): to walk through, once the formal word for pacing out a parish boundary.

definitions: Quick to perceive and understand; unusually alert to what is actually happening.
examples: A percipient junior editor noticed that the two anonymous manuscripts shared the same typewriter's dropped 'e.'
Kids are more percipient than we admit; she knew about the move before we said a word.
words.etymology: From Latin percipere, to grasp thoroughly, from per- plus capere, to take.

definitions: The rousing final section of a speech, where the speaker gathers everything for the emotional close; also, mockingly, any long-winded declamation.
examples: The defense attorney's peroration, delivered in a near whisper, did more than three days of testimony.
He turned a simple toast into a peroration on the meaning of friendship.
words.etymology: From Latin perorare, to speak through to the end, from per- plus orare, to speak.

definitions: Expressed so clearly that it cannot be misunderstood; transparent in presentation, said of writing and explanations rather than people.
examples: Among a shelf of impenetrable commentaries, hers is the one perspicuous guide, and generations of students have blessed her for it.
Make the instructions perspicuous enough for a tired parent at midnight.
words.etymology: From Latin perspicuus, transparent, from perspicere, to see through.

definitions: Holding on to a purpose or opinion with unshakable, sometimes annoying persistence.
examples: The pertinacious reporter called the ministry every morning for a year until someone finally cracked.
She's pertinacious about that theory despite three failed experiments.
words.etymology: From Latin per, thoroughly, plus tenax, gripping, from tenere, to hold: holding on all the way through.

definitions: The earthy smell that rises when rain hits dry ground.
examples: The first fat drops hit the dust and the smell of petrichor rolled up off the road like a memory.
She opened the windows after the storm just to let the petrichor in.
words.etymology: Coined by scientists in 1964 from Greek petra, stone, plus ichor, the fluid said to run in the veins of the gods.

definitions: A fierce public denunciation of a specific person, delivered with sustained oratorical fury.
examples: The shareholder rose at the annual meeting and delivered a philippic against the chairman that made the lawyers reach for their phones.
Her resignation letter was less a goodbye than a philippic.
words.etymology: From Demosthenes' speeches rallying Athens against Philip II of Macedon, a title Cicero later borrowed for his attacks on Antony.

definitions: Loud and reverberating with a mournful, beating quality, like bells, surf, or a cello.
examples: The plangent toll of the buoy bell reached them through the fog long before the shore did.
The film's plangent score does half the emotional work.
words.etymology: From Latin plangere, to beat the breast in mourning, so the sound carries grief in its root.

definitions: An utter coward, with a strong flavor of contempt and old-fashioned insult.
examples: The pamphlet branded the fleeing governor a poltroon who had abandoned his own militia.
Only a poltroon criticizes anonymously and hides from replies.
words.etymology: Via French from Italian poltrone, an idle coward, from poltro, couch or bed: one who lounges rather than fights.

definitions: Smugly proper, parading your small virtues and correcting everyone else's manners.
examples: The priggish prefect reported his own brother for an untucked shirt.
There's a priggish streak in how he polices everyone's grammar.
words.etymology: From prig, a 16th-century cant word of unknown origin that drifted from meaning thief to fop to self-righteous pedant.

definitions: Forcing everything to fit a rigid standard, mutilating whatever does not conform.
examples: The five-paragraph essay is a procrustean form: whatever the student actually thinks gets stretched or lopped to fit.
The budget template is procrustean; our project simply doesn't have five phases.
words.etymology: From Procrustes, the mythic Greek bandit who stretched short guests and amputated tall ones to fit his iron bed.

definitions: good-humored teasing and mockery, ribbing that stays friendly
examples: The wardroom's raillery about his landing was merciless and affectionate in equal measure.
Their raillery at dinner never quite tips into cruelty.
words.etymology: French railler, to tease or mock, the same root that gives us 'to rail,' though raillery kept the smile.

definitions: Careful step-by-step logical reasoning, the deliberate chain-of-deduction kind.
examples: Poe billed his detective stories as tales of ratiocination, puzzles solved by pure chains of inference.
No amount of ratiocination will tell you what the toddler did with the keys.
words.etymology: From Latin ratiocinari, to calculate or reason, from ratio, reckoning.

definitions: a meal, especially one framed with a certain formality or occasion
examples: After the funeral, the family shared a quiet repast of casseroles brought by half the county.
We enjoyed a light repast on the terrace before the concert.
words.etymology: Old French repaistre, to feed, from Latin pascere, to graze or feed.

definitions: A crust of granular white ice deposited by freezing fog or wind-driven mist.
examples: Rime grew inches thick on the summit cairn, sculpted into feathers by the prevailing wind.
By morning a thin rime coated the tents and guy lines.
words.etymology: From Old English hrim, frost, a word the Norse sagas share in their frost giants, the hrimthursar.

definitions: elaborately ornate in a light, curling, over-decorated way, fancy past the point of restraint
examples: Her apology email was positively rococo, five paragraphs of curlicued regret for a two-minute delay.
The cake was pure rococo, gold leaf on the gold leaf.
words.etymology: French, from rocaille, the shell-and-pebble ornament that covered eighteenth-century interiors in curls.

definitions: Possessing wisdom and judgment; often used half-seriously of humans as the allegedly wise species.
examples: The novel's only truly sapient character is the ship's computer, which spends the voyage trying to resign.
Some sapient soul had labeled every cable in the server room.
words.etymology: From Latin sapere, to taste and hence to be wise, the root of Homo sapiens.

definitions: Gloomy, brooding, and slow to smile, dark in both mood and often looks.
examples: The saturnine ferryman took their coins without a word and watched the far shore the whole crossing.
Even good news barely dented his saturnine expression.
words.etymology: From Saturn, the planet astrologers blamed for cold and gloomy temperaments in those born under it.

definitions: The loose, sliding rubble of broken rock on a mountainside.
examples: They descended the scree at a controlled slide, each step setting off little rivers of stone.
Wear boots with ankle support; the last mile is all scree.
words.etymology: From Old Norse skritha, a landslide, literally something that slides.

definitions: To race along quickly and lightly, driven by the wind, as low clouds and ships do.
examples: Torn gray clouds scudded over the headland faster than the gulls could fly against them.
Leaves scudded across the empty parking lot ahead of the front.
words.etymology: Of uncertain origin, in sailors' use since the 1500s for running fast before a gale.

definitions: Given to pompous moralizing in neat little maxims; wisdom delivered with a wagging finger.
examples: The commencement speaker grew sententious by minute three, dispensing proverbs like a fortune-cookie printer with tenure.
Her thank-you note was warm without being sententious.
words.etymology: From Latin sententia, an opinion or maxim: full of sentences, in the old sense of sayings.

definitions: Hissing, full of soft s-sounds, like whispers, escaping steam, or wind in dry grass.
examples: The tide withdrew with a long sibilant sigh over the shingle.
His sibilant whisper carried farther than he intended.
words.etymology: From Latin sibilare, to hiss, another word that performs its own meaning.

definitions: To smile in a coy, self-conscious, slightly silly way, often while fishing for approval.
examples: The contestant simpered at the judges through an answer she clearly hadn't prepared.
He simpered and shrugged instead of just admitting the mistake.
words.etymology: Attested in English since the 1500s, probably from a Scandinavian source; relatives survive in Norwegian dialect words for smirking.

definitions: a nickname that sticks, often affectionate or wry, that comes to stand in for the real name
examples: The rookie earned the sobriquet Blackout after tripping over the stadium's main power cable.
The city wears its sobriquet, the Big Easy, proudly.
words.etymology: French soubriquet, said to have first meant a teasing chuck under the chin, the little tap becoming a little name.

definitions: in a deliberately quiet voice, so only some can hear
said as an aside, unofficially
examples: The minister praised the treaty aloud and, sotto voce, told his aide to stall its ratification.
She added, sotto voce, that the deadline was never real.
words.etymology: Italian, literally 'under the voice': music's marking for hushed delivery, borrowed for conspiratorial asides.

definitions: Sea spray the wind rips off wave crests and carries through the air; also fine snow streaming off a ridge in high wind.
examples: Spindrift smoked off the wave crests and salted their faces a mile from open water.
The summit photo shows spindrift streaming off the ridge like white banners.
words.etymology: A Scots variant of spoondrift, from the old sailing verb spoon, to run before the wind, plus drift.

definitions: Bad-tempered and spiteful, full of irritable venom.
examples: The splenetic critic filed eight hundred words on why the play's intermission was its finest moment.
He fired off a splenetic reply before reading the whole email.
words.etymology: From the spleen, which old physiology held to be the seat of ill temper and black moods.

definitions: Sea foam, the froth churned up by breaking waves.
examples: Gouts of spume flew over the breakwater and spattered the harbor road.
The storm left lines of dirty spume along the tide mark.
words.etymology: From Latin spuma, foam.

definitions: extremely loud and powerful in voice, built for shouting over crowds
examples: The auctioneer's stentorian call carried over three hundred bidders and a thunderstorm.
The coach's stentorian whistle-and-bellow reached the far field unaided.
words.etymology: From Stentor, the herald in the Iliad whose voice equaled fifty men together.

definitions: A soft whispering or rustling sound, like wind through leaves or a room full of murmured conversation.
examples: A susurrus moved through the congregation as the will was read aloud.
The only sound in the tent was the susurrus of rain on nylon.
words.etymology: Straight from Latin susurrus, a whisper, a word built to sound like the thing it names.

definitions: devoted to luxury and sensual pleasure, self-indulgent on a grand scale
examples: The resort promised a sybaritic week: private plunge pools, pillow menus, a butler for the beach umbrella.
Their sybaritic weekend cost more than my rent.
words.etymology: From Sybaris, a Greek colony in southern Italy so notorious for soft living that its name became the insult.

definitions: A small, steep-sided mountain lake, usually carved by a glacier.
examples: They camped beside a black tarn that held the crags upside down in perfect stillness.
The trail climbs past two tarns before the final scramble.
words.etymology: From Old Norse tjorn, a small lake, carried into English by Norse settlers of the hill country.

definitions: to stall and avoid committing, playing for time until the situation decides itself
examples: The regent temporized for a decade, promising the crown to both nephews in alternating seasons.
Stop temporizing and give the contractor an answer.
words.etymology: French temporiser, from Latin tempus (time): to make time itself do your negotiating.

definitions: Deeply dark and shadowy, with a suggestion of the murky and obscure.
examples: The stair wound down into a tenebrous cellar where the lantern light simply gave up.
The novel's tenebrous atmosphere suits its unreliable narrator.
words.etymology: From Latin tenebrae, darkness.

definitions: A harsh, overbearing, scolding woman; a stock figure of shrewish bad temper.
examples: The innkeeper's wife played the termagant so convincingly that guests paid their bills before breakfast.
The play softens the termagant of the original into someone almost sympathetic.
words.etymology: From Termagant, a violent imaginary deity that medieval European mystery plays falsely attributed to Muslims; the ranting stage character became the word.

definitions: To slander someone, damaging their reputation with lies or distortions.
examples: Pamphleteers traduced the astronomer for a decade, and he answered them with nothing but better data.
He felt traduced by the meeting minutes, which recorded his caution as cowardice.
words.etymology: From Latin traducere, to lead across or parade in disgrace, from trans- plus ducere: to march someone's name through the streets.

definitions: To submit obsequiously to someone's will, caving in and currying favor.
examples: The provincial governors truckled to the capital, repealing local laws before anyone even asked.
He won't truckle to the loudest voice in the room, which I respect.
words.etymology: From the truckle bed, a low bed on casters stored under a bigger one: to truckle was to sleep in the lower bed, hence to take the inferior position.

definitions: To howl or wail in long, wavering cries, whether in grief, celebration, or the voice of wolves and wind.
examples: Somewhere beyond the firelight a jackal ululated, and the horses stamped in the dark.
The wedding guests ululated as the couple emerged.
words.etymology: From Latin ululare, to howl, a word that imitates the sound it names.

definitions: Excessive pride in yourself and hunger for admiration, vanity elevated to a vice.
examples: The general's vainglory demanded a triumphal arch for a battle that had been mostly a rainstorm.
The memoir drips with vainglory on every page.
words.etymology: From the medieval Latin phrase vana gloria, empty glory, one of the classic vices catalogued by early Christian moralists.

definitions: To boast about something, displaying or praising it with conspicuous pride.
examples: The brochure vaunted the resort's private beach, which turned out to be a gravel strip with a rope.
Its much-vaunted security system failed on day one.
words.etymology: From Late Latin vanitare, to talk emptily, from vanus, empty: boasting as the sound of emptiness.

definitions: The blue-green crust that weathering forms on copper, brass, and bronze.
examples: Verdigris streaked the cathedral dome, a green map of a century of rain.
The old door knocker was crusted with verdigris until she polished it back to gold.
words.etymology: From Old French vert de Grece, green of Greece, the medieval name for the pigment.

definitions: A loud, domineering, aggressive woman; in older use, a woman of heroic, warrior-like strength.
examples: The market virago drove off two inspectors and a rival stallholder before her tea went cold.
Elizabethan writers used virago admiringly as often as not.
words.etymology: From Latin virago, a man-like female warrior, built on vir, man, the root of virile.

definitions: Bitterly abusive in language, scolding with sustained verbal violence.
examples: The dictator's vituperative broadcast named enemies for two full hours.
The comment section turned vituperative within minutes.
words.etymology: From Latin vituperare, to blame or disparage, from vitium, fault, plus parare, to furnish: to load someone with faults.

definitions: a complete reversal of position or policy, an about-face executed in public
examples: The minister's volte-face on the dam left allies who had defended the project for years stranded mid-argument.
The company did a volte-face on remote work within a single quarter.
words.etymology: French, from Italian voltafaccia, volta (turn) plus faccia (face): turning to face the other way.

definitions: To make your way somewhere slowly and indirectly, almost always in the phrase wend one's way.
examples: The funeral procession wended its way through lanes hung with wet lilac.
We wended our way home through the back streets to avoid the parade.
words.etymology: From Old English wendan, to turn, whose old past tense went was adopted by the verb go.

definitions: Looking utterly miserable, wearing sorrow all over your face and posture.
examples: The woebegone puppy sat in the rain beside the empty food bowl like a tragedy in miniature.
He came back from the exam looking woebegone.
words.etymology: From Middle English wo begon, beset by woe: begon meant surrounded, so the word pictures grief closing in on all sides.

definitions: a position where you must act and every available move makes your situation worse
examples: The startup was in zugzwang: raising money meant brutal dilution, waiting meant missing payroll.
It's zugzwang, whichever answer he gives the committee, one side quits.
words.etymology: German Zug (move) plus Zwang (compulsion), a chess term for the agony of being forced to move.

definitions: Spiritual apathy and listlessness, a torpor of the soul that makes everything feel pointless; the ancestor of the sin of sloth.
examples: The desert monks called acedia the noonday demon, the flat hour when prayer and labor both felt absurd.
It wasn't burnout exactly, more a creeping acedia about the whole career.
words.etymology: From Greek akedia, from a plus kedos, care: literally the absence of caring.

definitions: In a wild, uncontrolled frenzy; to run amok is to rampage beyond all restraint.
examples: A single loose goat ran amok through the farmers market, redistributing an entire stall of heirloom tomatoes.
The rumor ran amok before anyone could correct it.
words.etymology: From Malay amuk, a murderous frenzy: European traders' accounts of frenzied attacks carried the word into English.

definitions: Self-esteem that depends on how others see you, vanity fed by comparison and opinion.
examples: Losing the award stung his amour-propre far more than his finances.
Rousseau argued that amour-propre, unlike simple self-love, is born only in society.
words.etymology: French for self-love; Rousseau sharpened it into a term for esteem that lives in other people's eyes.

definitions: A pointed critical remark; censure delivered with formal disapproval.
examples: The review's animadversions on his prose style stung longer than its verdict on his argument.
She endured her aunt's animadversions on modern parenting through two full courses.
words.etymology: From Latin animum advertere, to turn the mind toward, which drifted from mere noticing to noticing with disapproval.

definitions: Necessarily true and demonstrably so, beyond possible dispute; also said of a person's manner when they assert things that way.
examples: Geometry gave philosophers their model of apodictic truth, claims that could not be otherwise.
His emails have an apodictic tone that turns every suggestion into a decree.
words.etymology: From Greek apodeiknynai, to demonstrate or show forth, from apo- plus deiknynai, to show.

definitions: the trick of raising a subject by declaring you won't mention it
examples: By saying 'I refuse to discuss my opponent's arrest,' the candidate performed a textbook apophasis.
The eulogy's apophasis, 'we needn't recall his temper,' recalled it perfectly.
words.etymology: Greek apophanai, to say no or deny (apo- plus phanai, to speak): assertion smuggled inside a denial.

definitions: A genuine impasse in reasoning, where every available path leads to contradiction; also a rhetorical pose of being at a loss.
examples: The dialogue ends not in a definition but in aporia, with Socrates cheerfully declaring them all still ignorant.
Faced with two irreconcilable readings of the clause, the court reached honest aporia.
words.etymology: From Greek aporia, impassability, from a-, without, plus poros, passage: a road with no way through.

definitions: Lying or moving crosswise over something, and by extension standing in opposition to it.
examples: A fallen fir lay athwart the trail, beaded with rain and already growing moss.
His ambitions ran athwart the committee's plans, and only one side could win.
words.etymology: From a-, on, plus thwart, from Old Norse thvert, across, the same crossing idea as a boat's thwart.

definitions: Gloomy and bad-tempered, melancholic with a bitter, irritable edge.
examples: The atrabilious lighthouse keeper logged each sunset as another disappointment.
Reviews of his later, atrabilious essays split between brilliant and unbearable.
words.etymology: From Latin atra bilis, black bile, a direct translation of the Greek melancholia: the humor blamed for dark moods.

definitions: fond of drinking alcohol, given to the bottle, said with a wink
examples: Their bibulous book club had long since stopped pretending the novels were the point.
It was a bibulous reunion, three toasts before the appetizers.
words.etymology: Latin bibere, to drink, via bibulus, originally said of sponges and soil that soak up liquid.

definitions: Thick with bushes and small trees, pleasantly overgrown and shady.
examples: The path dove into a bosky hollow where the afternoon suddenly became evening.
The garden's bosky corner is where the kids build their forts.
words.etymology: From Middle English bosk, a variant of bush.

definitions: Mist or fog, especially the cold gray haze of winter.
examples: The city woke wrapped in brume, church spires floating unmoored above the streets.
A low brume hung over the fields until nearly noon.
words.etymology: From French brume, mist, from Latin bruma, the winter solstice, so the word carries winter inside it.

definitions: insulting, contemptuous treatment, scorn delivered with rank behind it
examples: He bore the board's contumely with a stillness that unnerved them more than any protest.
Hamlet lists 'the proud man's contumely' among life's unbearable burdens.
words.etymology: Latin contumelia, insult, possibly related to tumere (to swell): abuse puffed up with arrogance.

definitions: A conceited, foppish man, absurdly proud of his looks and cleverness.
examples: The coxcomb paused at every mirror in the gallery, admiring himself past the Rembrandts.
Eighteenth-century satire is thick with coxcombs and their ruined tailors' bills.
words.etymology: From cock's comb, the red crest on a jester's cap: the fool's headgear became the vain fool himself.

definitions: to cheat or trick someone, usually by winning their trust first
examples: The stranger cozened the innkeeper out of a horse with nothing but a forged seal and good manners.
He was cozened into signing away the mineral rights.
words.etymology: Origin uncertain, possibly from Italian cozzone, a horse trader, a profession whose reputation did the semantic work.

definitions: To crackle or pop in a series of small sharp sounds, like burning twigs or new frost underfoot.
examples: The bonfire crepitated and spat sparks into the cold, and the children stepped back laughing.
Fresh snow crepitated under their boots in the silence.
words.etymology: From Latin crepitare, to crackle, the frequentative of crepare, to rattle or creak.

definitions: The state of no longer being used, the quiet obsolescence things fall into when everyone stops bothering.
examples: The canal locks had sunk into desuetude, gates rotted open, towpath lost under bramble.
The bylaw fell into desuetude decades ago, though it was never repealed.
words.etymology: From Latin desuescere, to become unaccustomed, from de plus suescere, to become used to.

definitions: Arguing to win rather than to find truth; treating debate as combat where victory is the only point.
examples: The debate club turned him eristic; he would defend either side of anything with equal fervor and equal indifference.
Online forums reward eristic tactics over honest inquiry.
words.etymology: From Greek eris, strife, the name of the goddess whose golden apple started the Trojan War.

definitions: Pale, drawn, and feeble from lack of light, like a plant grown in a cellar, or anything drained of vigor.
examples: Etiolated seedlings strained toward the one grimy window of the basement flat.
After a decade of committee edits, the proposal emerged etiolated, all its boldness bred out.
words.etymology: From French etioler, to blanch a plant by growing it in darkness.

definitions: with the full weight of one's official authority, pronounced as beyond question
examples: The editor ruled ex cathedra that the serial comma would outlive them all.
He delivers restaurant opinions ex cathedra, as if appeals were impossible.
words.etymology: Latin, 'from the chair': the pope speaking from the throne of Saint Peter, whose formal doctrinal pronouncements are held infallible.
