definitions: for lack of anything better, settled on only because superior options were unavailable
examples: Stranded in the airport hotel, they dined, faute de mieux, on vending-machine pretzels and minibar wine.
I took the aisle seat faute de mieux.
words.etymology: French, literally for lack of better.

definitions: To laugh or grin mockingly at someone, jeering with your face as much as your voice.
examples: The pit crowd fleered at the fallen duelist until the marshal cleared the yard.
The older boys fleered at his homemade costume.
words.etymology: Middle English, probably from a Scandinavian source; Norwegian dialect flire, to giggle or grin, is a likely relative.

definitions: Willfully contrary and disobedient, habitually turning against what is asked of you.
examples: The chronicle complains of a froward apprentice who salted the abbot's ink and hid the bell rope.
The old manual warns against froward oxen and froward servants in the same sentence.
words.etymology: From Old English fromweard, turned away: built from fro plus ward, it is the literal opposite of toward.

definitions: pretentious, inflated language, high-sounding stuffing
originally, a coarse cotton cloth
examples: The dedication speech was pure fustian, empires and destinies invoked over a parking garage.
Elizabethan critics mocked playwrights for their fustian.
words.etymology: Named for a coarse cotton padding cloth, Medieval Latin fustaneum: fabric for stuffing garments became the word for stuffed speech.

definitions: Swaggering boastfulness, extravagant big talk.
examples: His gasconade about wrestling the alligator shrank, under questioning, to having once seen one from a bridge.
The press release was pure gasconade.
words.etymology: From Gascony in southwestern France, whose natives were stereotyped as flamboyant boasters.

definitions: Of or belonging to winter.
examples: The park in its hibernal bareness showed bones no summer visitor ever saw.
The lodge switches to its hibernal schedule after the first heavy snow.
words.etymology: From Latin hibernus, wintry, the root that also gives us hibernate.

definitions: To treat an abstract concept as a really existing thing; reification in its formal philosophical dress.
examples: Critics charged that the school had hypostatized 'the economy,' converting a description of exchanges into a deity with moods.
Don't hypostatize 'team morale'; there are just twelve tired people.
words.etymology: From Greek hypostasis, that which stands beneath, from hypo- plus histanai, to stand: to give an idea its own footing in reality.

definitions: A bare assertion offered with no proof beyond the speaker's authority: believe it because I said so.
examples: The expert's report was one long ipse dixit, conclusions stacked on credentials with no data underneath.
The rule against blue ink is pure ipse dixit; nobody can say where it came from.
words.etymology: Latin for 'he himself said it,' the formula Pythagoras's disciples used to end arguments by citing the master.

definitions: an utterly servile flatterer, groveling past the point of self-respect
examples: The court's lickspittles applauded the emperor's poetry before he finished reading the title.
He surrounded himself with lickspittles and called it loyalty.
words.etymology: Lick plus spittle: a compound so literal it needs no story, one who would lick up another's spit.

definitions: Laborious study done late into the night; also, with a smirk, the overwrought writing such study produces.
examples: The archive preserves his lucubrations, thousands of midnight pages annotating a poet no one else read.
Spare me your latest lucubration on the office seating chart.
words.etymology: From Latin lucubrare, to work by lamplight, from lux, light: study done by the lamp.

definitions: Drawing knowledge out of a person by questioning, as if delivering ideas they already carried; the Socratic method's proper name.
examples: Her tutoring was maieutic: she never told him the theorem, only asked questions until he gave birth to it himself.
Good therapy is maieutic; the insight has to be yours.
words.etymology: From Greek maieutikos, of midwifery, from maia, midwife: Socrates called himself a midwife of ideas.

definitions: Of or belonging to the early morning.
examples: He kept matutinal habits, walking the harbor wall before the gulls had finished arguing over the night's scraps.
Her matutinal coffee ritual is not to be interrupted.
words.etymology: From Latin matutinus, of the morning, from Matuta, the Roman goddess of dawn.

definitions: To rain in a fine, misty drizzle.
examples: It mizzled all across the moor, too soft for umbrellas and too wet to ignore.
It's not really raining, just mizzling; bring a hat.
words.etymology: From Middle English misellen, related to Low German and Dutch dialect words for drizzle such as miezelen.

definitions: With the necessary changes made; signaling that a comparison holds once you swap the obvious details.
examples: The argument for term limits applies, mutatis mutandis, to tenured chairs and standing committees.
Our onboarding checklist works for contractors too, mutatis mutandis.
words.etymology: Latin for 'with the things changed that must be changed,' an ablative absolute from mutare, to change.

definitions: Plain absence of knowledge; not-knowing, without the moral sting of ignorance.
examples: The report distinguished the board's nescience from its negligence: they could not have known, and also never asked.
On the subject of opera I claim complete nescience.
words.etymology: From Latin ne-, not, plus scire, to know: the exact mirror of science.

definitions: To scold or rebuke someone harshly and at length.
examples: The headmistress objurgated the fourth form for a full quarter hour over one missing library book.
He objurgated the interns publicly, which told us more about him than about them.
words.etymology: From Latin objurgare, to chide, from ob plus jurgare, to quarrel or sue, itself built on jus, law: scolding with legal thoroughness.

definitions: to deceive with technically true statements and evasions, bargaining in bad faith
examples: The witch's prophecies paltered with Macbeth, true in every word and false in every promise.
The vendor paltered about the warranty, honest sentence by sentence, misleading in sum.
words.etymology: Origin obscure, kin perhaps to paltry: dealing in trifles that add up to a lie.

definitions: to travel or wander from place to place, especially on a long, meandering journey
examples: After selling the firm, she peregrinated through the Balkans for a year with one duffel bag and no return ticket.
We peregrinated across three arrondissements looking for that bakery.
words.etymology: Latin peregrinari, to travel abroad, from per plus ager, through the fields, the same root that gives pilgrim.

definitions: Intensely, excessively impassioned, burning hotter than the occasion warrants.
examples: His perfervid defense of the semicolon ran to eleven pages and two historical grudges.
The rally's perfervid rhetoric worried even some supporters.
words.etymology: From Latin per, thoroughly, plus fervidus, boiling: heated all the way through.

definitions: To weigh a matter carefully in the mind; to ponder, in a word now worn mostly as antique costume.
examples: Perpend, says Polonius, and four centuries of audiences have braced for the worst.
Give me the weekend to perpend the offer.
words.etymology: From Latin perpendere, to weigh exactly, from per- plus pendere, to weigh, the root of pendulum and pound.

definitions: light, mocking banter that treats serious things frivolously
examples: The diplomats conducted the crisis in elegant persiflage while their staffs drafted ultimatums downstairs.
Under all his persiflage there was real worry.
words.etymology: French persifler, to banter or mock, built on siffler (to whistle): teasing with a whistle in it.

definitions: Self-righteously hypocritical, making a show of virtue while missing its substance.
examples: There was something pharisaical in the tycoon's lectures on thrift, delivered from the deck of his third yacht.
Her pharisaical concern for the rules evaporates when they inconvenience her.
words.etymology: From the Pharisees of the New Testament, portrayed there as scrupulous about ritual law while neglecting mercy.

definitions: A vain, chattering dandy who preens like a parrot in fine feathers.
examples: The court popinjay changed cloaks three times a day and opinions three times an hour.
He dismissed the pundit as a popinjay with a good tailor.
words.etymology: From Old French papingay, parrot, ultimately from Arabic babbagha: the strutting, squawking bird became the strutting, squawking man.

definitions: A formal introductory essay laying the groundwork for a longer study; the throat-clearing before the argument proper.
examples: The eighty-page prolegomenon defines terms so exhaustively that the actual theory arrives like an afterthought.
Consider this memo a prolegomenon to the full proposal.
words.etymology: From Greek prolegein, to say beforehand: the thing said before the main matter, plural prolegomena.

definitions: To flow with a soft rippling, babbling sound, as a small stream over stones.
examples: A brook purled somewhere below the path, invisible under the ferns but never silent.
Water purled from the fountain in the courtyard all afternoon.
words.etymology: Likely from a Scandinavian source, compare Norwegian purla, to bubble up, a word that mimics the water it describes.

definitions: The essential whatness of a thing, what makes it precisely itself; also, in an older use, a hairsplitting quibble.
examples: Her portraits catch the quiddity of a face, the one unrepeatable arrangement that photographs somehow miss.
The essay circles the quiddity of jazz without ever pinning it down.
words.etymology: From medieval Latin quidditas, whatness, coined from quid, what, by the Scholastics.

definitions: exquisitely rare or obscure, chosen to display refined taste few could follow
examples: His playlist was so recherché that even the record store owner had to look two of the artists up.
The menu's recherché ingredients needed footnotes.
words.etymology: French, past participle of rechercher, to seek out carefully: the sought-after thing, prized because it took searching.

definitions: extravagant, boastful ranting, bragging inflated to theatrical scale
examples: The warlord's radio rodomontade promised victories his army had stopped believing in.
Strip away the rodomontade and his record shows two wins.
words.etymology: From Rodomonte, the thunderously boastful Saracen king in Ariosto's Orlando Furioso.

definitions: A shallow smattering of knowledge paraded as expertise; the specialty of the confident amateur.
examples: The panel's resident contrarian offered pure sciolism, a semester's worth of Wikipedia delivered with a professor's cadence.
Reading one article on nutrition and lecturing the table is textbook sciolism.
words.etymology: From Latin sciolus, a smatterer, the diminutive of scius, knowing, from scire, to know: one who knows a little.

definitions: Dry and withered, browned off by drought or the turn of the year.
examples: They rode all day through sere grassland the color of a lion's back.
By late August the hills go sere and the fire danger signs go red.
words.etymology: From Old English sear, withered, close kin to the verb sear, to scorch dry.

definitions: groomed and turned out with meticulous, polished elegance, not a thread out of place
examples: She arrived soigné as ever, silk scarf knotted just so, though her flight had landed forty minutes earlier.
The hotel bar draws a soigné crowd on Fridays.
words.etymology: French, past participle of soigner, to care for: appearance that has been cared for down to the detail.

definitions: To make a soft moaning, sighing, or rushing sound, as wind does in trees.
examples: All night the pines soughed above the cabin, a sound like the sea deciding something.
You could hear the wind sough through the telephone wires.
words.etymology: From Old English swogan, to make a rushing or roaring sound.

definitions: studied nonchalance, the art of making difficult things look effortless by hiding all the effort
examples: His blazer looked thrown on, but the sprezzatura was engineered: every rumple had been rehearsed in a mirror.
Her keynote had real sprezzatura, months of prep passing as improvisation.
words.etymology: Coined by Castiglione in his 1528 Book of the Courtier for the courtier's duty to conceal all art and effort.

definitions: A lament composed for the dead, whether sung or written as verse; grief given formal musical or poetic shape.
examples: The wind in the wires above the battlefield made a thin threnody that no one mentioned and everyone heard.
The composer's last symphony is a threnody for his drowned son.
words.etymology: From Greek threnos, a lament, plus oide, song: literally a grief-song.

definitions: The ringing and jingling of bells, especially the lingering sound after they are struck.
examples: The tintinnabulation of the goat bells followed them all the way up the terraced hillside.
Poe built a whole poem around the tintinnabulation of sleigh bells.
words.etymology: From Latin tintinnabulum, a bell, from tinnire, to ring, and made famous by Poe's poem The Bells.

definitions: a person with a heroic appetite, a dedicated and unembarrassed heavy eater
examples: A legendary trencherman, the mayor once judged the pie contest by finishing all fourteen entries.
You'll want a second brisket, my brother-in-law is a serious trencherman.
words.etymology: From trencher, the slab of stale bread or wood that served as a medieval plate: the man who clears the trencher.

definitions: an arbitrary command from on high, issued without appeal or explanation
examples: The new owner's first ukase abolished remote work in a company he had visited once.
Policy arrived by ukase, never by discussion.
words.etymology: Russian ukaz, a decree, from ukazat' (to show, to decree): the tsar's word with the force of law.

definitions: excessively devoted to one's wife, doting past the point observers find dignified
examples: The uxorious senator cleared every vote, menu, and necktie with his wife, to his staff's despair.
He's uxorious in the sweetest way, her opinion settles everything.
words.etymology: Latin uxor, wife: a husband defined entirely by the word for wife.

definitions: The faintest degree of wanting: a wish so weak it never even tries to become action.
examples: His plan to learn Greek remained a velleity for thirty years, renewed each January and abandoned by the sixth.
I have a velleity to reorganize the garage, which is to say I will not.
words.etymology: From Latin velle, to wish, coined by the Scholastics as velleitas for willing at its lowest intensity.

definitions: Of or happening in the evening; in botany and zoology, opening or active at dusk.
examples: The vespertine chorus of frogs started up the moment the sun touched the treeline.
Evening primrose is vespertine, unfolding just as the garden empties.
words.etymology: From Latin vesper, evening, the same word as the evening star and the evening service of vespers.

definitions: A weary sadness about the state of the world, the melancholy gap between life as it is and as it should be.
examples: He read three newspapers before breakfast and carried the resulting weltschmerz around like a briefcase.
The album trades the band's old rage for a gentler weltschmerz.
words.etymology: From German Welt, world, plus Schmerz, pain: world-pain, coined in the era of Romantic literature.
